text.content:
Level: 1
Duration: ½ session
Locations: The PCs' home towns
Type: Roleplay introduction, table-round character moments
Tone: Grim duty, communal desperation, old law invoked in new crisis

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 0 | Level: 1 | VP into Level: 0
Campaign start - heroes begin at Level 1.

Overview

Before the campaign’s main machinery begins, the ancient call goes out under High King Harald’s Danelaw: a Konungs-ferð—a king’s-journey duty, invoked in desperation. The last time this law was used was three generations ago, during the Second Barrow-War. Its invocation now tells every jarl in Svellheim that the king is out of options.

The call reaches multiple towns (the PCs' homes) through riders, skalds, stamped notices, or lantern signals. Each community chooses (or pressures) a local hero to answer. The chosen are told to make for Hrafnborg—because contracts, rations, and authority still exist there.

This beat establishes:

  • Svellheim is freezing. The world is ending slowly.

  • The PCs are not chosen for glory—they are chosen because someone had to go.

  • The law still has weight, even when everything else is failing.

The Call

Run this as a quick table-round introduction. Each player describes:

  • How the Konungs-ferð reached their character’s town (rider, skald, lantern signal, sealed letter with the raven-seal).

  • How their community "volunteered" them (Thing vote, hearth-oath called in, a debt owed, a Quiet Warden’s quiet suggestion, a parent’s sacrifice).

  • What they packed (one personal item that matters to them—this becomes a character hook later).

The call is formal, ancient, and heavy with law:

"By the Danelaw of High King Harald, bearer of the Cold Iron Crown, a Konungs-ferð is proclaimed. Let each settlement of loyal standing send forth one champion to Hrafnborg, there to serve the realm’s need in this time of shadow and frost."

The words are old. The desperation is new.

Running the Selection

Let the table roleplay why each hero was chosen. Give each player 2–3 minutes to set their scene.

Prompts for the local jarl or hall-leader:

  • "You’re too good to waste here. Go make my name bigger."

  • "If you die in the fjords, I won’t have to feed you."

  • "You owe me for the winter I saved you from. This is the price."

  • "They asked for a warrior. I’m sending a problem."

  • "You’re the only one who can speak to strangers without starting a blood-feud."

  • "The Warden said your name. I don’t argue with the Wardens."

What the PCs Know

  • The Fimbulwinter is three years old and getting worse.

  • The dead are rising—not everywhere, but more often, and closer to towns.

  • Hrafnborg is the largest settlement still standing. The king is there. Rations are there. Law is there.

  • A Konungs-ferð is a serious thing. Refusing it stains a family’s name for a generation.

What the PCs Don’t Know

  • Why the Konungs-ferð was really called.

  • That the Ever-Embers are dying.

  • That the Ember-Keepers are dead.

  • That they are being chosen specifically because they are unknown—outsiders the king can trust because no one in his court has had time to compromise them.

GM Notes

ðŸ“ Pacing and Tone

This beat should feel like the beginning of a journey no one wants to make. The PCs are not heroes yet—they are burdens their communities can afford to lose, debts that needed paying, or good people in bad circumstances who couldn’t say no.

Do not rush this. The emotional foundation of the campaign is laid here. When the PCs look back on this moment from Beat 9, standing outside a besieged Hrafnborg, the contrast should hit like a hammer.

If a player struggles with the "why me?" prompt, offer one: "Your community’s healer says the dead whisper your bloodline’s name. Better you go than they come here."

ðŸ“ Connecting to Character Options

If your players chose Svellheim-specific ancestries, cultures, or careers during character creation, tie the Konungs-ferð to those choices:

  • Hearthborn: The local Ever-Ember flickered when the call arrived. The elders took it as a sign.

  • Frostblood: The community doesn’t trust what you are. Sending you away solves two problems.

  • Kin of the Deep: Something stirred in the old mine. The foreman says you know too much to stay.

  • Warden-touched: A Quiet Warden visited your door at dawn. "The dead are choosing sides. Time you did too."

Beat 1 — Milestone Exp Summary

No VP-earning encounters this beat. Total: 0 VP.

Transition to Beat 2

Once each player has described their departure, compress time:

You leave behind the only warmth you’ve ever known. The roads are bad—worse than last year, worse than the year before. The mile-markers are buried. The lanterns are dark. But the direction is clear: south toward the coast, toward Hrafnborg.

You are not the only one on the road.

The PCs begin to scene each other on the Lantern Road. Proceed to Beat 2: The Road to Hrafnborg.

Level: 1
Duration: ½–1 session
Locations: The Lantern Road, a wayside inn (optional), a graveyard ruin outside Hrafnborg
Type: Travel montage (Easy), social introduction, combat scene
Tone: Cold fellowship, growing dread, first blood

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 0 | Level: 1 | VP into Level: 0

Overview

The PCs travel to Hrafnborg along the failing Lantern Road. On the way, they meet each other—strangers bound by the same grim duty. They share a road, a fire, and their suspicions.

On the last night, just outside Hrafnborg’s outer walls, the party passes an old church ruin with a frozen graveyard. The dead rise. This is the PCs' first combat scene and their first proof that the crisis is real.

This beat establishes:

  • The PCs as a group (from suspicion to necessity).

  • Travel in Svellheim is dangerous and expensive.

  • The dead are rising, and they remember.

Part 1: The Road

milestones — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Easy | VP Award: 1 VP on success / 0 VP on failure

Run the journey as a short Montage Test (Easy difficulty, 2–3 days of travel).

Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Easy (adjusted) 4 4

Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate the Lantern Road Reason Stay on failing roads, read buried markers, avoid dead-end detours.
Endure the cold Might Resist frostbite, carry gear through snowdrifts, ford frozen streams.
Find shelter at night Intuition Locate wind-breaks, abandoned crofts, or natural shelters.
Avoid trouble on the road Agility Slip past bandit camps, desperate refugees, or rival groups.

Montage Outcomes

  • Total Success: Arrive rested and supplied. No Heat loss. Earn 1 Victory.

  • Partial Success: Arrive tired. Lose 1 day’s rations (Heat) or one piece of minor equipment is damaged (Weight).

  • Total Failure: Arrive exhausted and rattled. One PC has a condition (frostbitten or exhausted). Lose 2 days' rations. Director gains 2 additional Malice for the graveyard scene.

Part 2: Meeting on the Road

During the montage, the PCs scene each other. Let them introduce themselves in character.

Start with suspicion, necessity, and shared hardship. Maybe they camp together. Maybe they share a fire at a roadside waystation.

Conversation starters:

  • "You answering the Konungs-ferð too? You poor bastard."

  • "I’ve got half a fire. You’ve got half a fire. Together that’s almost warm."

  • "Where are you from? …Never heard of it. Probably still standing, then."

Optional: The Burnt Peat (Tavern Introduction)

If you want a classic "you all meet in a tavern" opener, place a waystation inn halfway to Hrafnborg.

  • Name: The Burnt Peat

  • Warmth costs extra. (1 iron-token for a seat near the fire.)

  • The locals are wary of anyone answering the Konungs-ferð. ("You poor bastards. The king’s scraping the barrel now.")

  • The PCs can share a table, a fire, and their reasons for coming.

This gives you a social scene before the violence. It’s also a chance to introduce minor color NPCs (a Lantern Road courier, an Ashwright peat-seller, a Quiet Warden who says nothing but watches the PCs with unsettling calm).

Part 3: The Graveyard (combat scene)

milestones — Type: Combat | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

On the second or third night, just outside Hrafnborg’s outer walls, the party passes an old church ruin. The structure is half-collapsed. The graveyard is frozen, the headstones cracked and leaning.

The ruin looms against the grey sky—stone ribs of a long-dead faith. The graveyard sprawls around it: tilted stones, frozen earth, and something wrong in the air.

The cold here is different. It doesn’t just chill your skin. It pulls.

Then you hear it: the scrape of bone on frozen ground.

Scene: Draugr Ambush

Difficulty: Moderate (Level 1 PCs)
Scene Value: Tune to party size (3 heroes ≈ EV 12–16)

Enemies:

  • 3–4 Draugr Thralls (Level 1 Minions)

  • 1 Barrow-Warden (Level 2 Standard)

Terrain:

  • Frozen ground (difficult terrain in patches)

  • Cracked headstones (cover, 1 feet (2 m) high)

  • Ruined church walls (partial shelter, 2 feet wide, line-of-sight blocker)

Lighting: Dim (torches/lanterns required; attacks without a light source have a second chance (two rolls, take worst))

Special Rule: The Dead Remember the Law

When a draugr is reduced to 0 Toughness, it whispers a name or an accusation before crumbling. This is narrative color, but it foreshadows later revelations:

  • "You broke guest-right…"

  • "The Ember died because of you…"

  • "Kaelen’s name is ash…"

  • "The wolf waits beneath the root…"

Let the PCs react. Do not explain these whispers. They are seeds planted for Beats 7, 8, and 9.

Stat Blocks

Use the following entities from the Svellheim Entities module:

  • Draugr Thrall (Level 1 Minion)
  • Barrow-Warden (Level 2 Standard) — Carries a Frost-Etched Barrow-Blade

Aftermath

After the fight:

  • Let the PCs search the bodies. They find a Ember-Keeper's Burial Coin stamped with the ember-mark (a seven-pointed flame). If any PC recognizes it (medium Reason test), they know it’s an Ember-Keeper’s token—old, worn, and shouldn’t be in a common graveyard.

  • One PC might be touched by the Barrow-Warden’s accusation. ("You will fail them, too…") This is narrative, not mechanical—but let it land.

  • The PCs can see Hrafnborg’s walls in the distance. Lights. Smoke. Bells.

⚔ï¸ Loot

  • Ember-Keeper's Burial Coin — Found on the Barrow-Warden’s body. A medium Reason test identifies it as an Ember-Keeper’s token.
  • Frost-Etched Barrow-Blade — Taken from the Barrow-Warden. The runes are Old Norrœnt and reference “the law of the grave.”

The last draugr crumbles to frost and ash. For a moment, the only sound is your breathing and the wind through the ruin’s ribs.

Then, from the south, you hear bells. Hrafnborg.

You’ve arrived.

Beat 2 — Milestone Exp Summary

EventDifficultyVP Award
Part 1: Lantern Road MontageEasy1 VP on success
Part 3: Draugr AmbushModerate1 VP on victory
Beat Total (Mandatory)up to 2 VP

Transition to Beat 3

The PCs approach Hrafnborg’s outer gates. They are processed by Chainwarden clerks—names taken, debts recorded, Konungs-ferð papers stamped. They are directed to modest quarters in a fortified hall. Fed. Told to wait.

The waiting feels wrong. Proceed to Beat 3: The Quiet Summons.

Level: 1
Duration: 1 session
Location: Hrafnborg — the King’s quarters
Key NPCs: High King Harald, Gragnir (Ash-Walker), Dreyfus (the hound)
Type: Roleplay, lore delivery, mission briefing
Tone: Paranoia, secrecy, the weight of impossible choices

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 2 | Level: 1 | VP into Level: 2

Overview

The PCs have arrived in Hrafnborg after the graveyard fight. They expect processing, maybe assignments, perhaps even rest. Instead, they are kept in polite custody all day. Late in the evening, High King Harald arrives personally—no heralds, no guards—and leads them to his private sitting room.

Inside, with only a grey hound as witness, Harald reveals the truth: the Ever-Embers are dying, the Keepers are murdered, and Svellheim’s warmth is failing.

Gragnir appears from the shadows with the first step: find Lew Tosferd in Stairwatch, who can find Kaelen. The session ends with the PCs accepting the mission and Dreyfus having heard every word.

Part 1: Polite Custody

The PCs are ushered into a modest but warm chamber in one of Hrafnborg’s fortified halls. Guards bring food—salt pork, hard bread, watered ale—and stoke the brazier. They are told to rest. To wait. That the King will "receive them soon."

Hours pass. Morning becomes afternoon. Afternoon becomes dusk.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Building Tension

This waiting period is meant to feel wrong. Let the players describe what their characters do. Some may try to leave—politely discourage them with guards who are unfailingly courteous but immovable.

"The King’s orders, friend. For your safety."

Let tests (easy Reason or Intuition test) or passive observations reveal:

  • The guards change every two hours. They’re professional, wary, and won’t answer questions.

  • No one else is being treated this way. Other Konungs-ferð arrivals are processed through the Chainwardens' ledgers and released into the city.

  • The PCs can hear distant alarm bells twice during the day—brief, then silenced. (These are wall attacks the King will mention later.)

Pacing tip: If the players get restless, compress time. "Another hour passes. Then another. The light outside turns grey, then dark. And then—the door opens."

Part 2: The King Comes

The door opens quietly. No fanfare. No herald.

A man steps inside—broad-shouldered, greying at the temples, wearing a simple wool tunic under a fur-lined cloak. His eyes are sunken, and his hands bear fresh ink stains. Behind him, the hallway is empty.

"Come," he says, voice low. "And speak softly."

He doesn’t wait for questions. He turns and walks.

This is High King Harald. PCs who grew up in Svellheim’s towns will recognize him from coins, carvings, or stories. He looks older than his reputation. Tired.

Let the PCs follow or hesitate. If they ask questions aloud, Harald puts a finger to his lips and gestures: follow.

The Walk

Harald leads them through cold stone corridors. PCs with sharp eyes (easy Reason or Intuition test) notice:

  • No other guards in this section.

  • The torches are spaced farther apart than usual.

  • The route avoids the main halls.

When they reach the King’s private door, there are no guards. This is unusual. In Hrafnborg, even minor nobles post at least one huscarl.

Harald unlocks the door himself, ushers the PCs inside, and locks it behind them.

Part 3: The Sitting Room

The room is warm—perhaps the warmest you’ve felt since the graveyard. A low fire burns in a stone hearth. Furs cover the floor. A table holds a map weighted with cold daggers. Shelves line the walls: scrolls, wax tablets, a child’s wooden sword.

The room is empty. No advisors. No guards. No servants.

On the floor near the fire, a large grey hound raises its head. Its eyes are pale, almost silver. It watches you without blinking.

Harald closes the door and leans against it. For a moment, he just breathes.

The hound is Dreyfus. Let the PCs react. If they try to speak to the dog, it does not respond—but it watches. If anyone uses magic, makes an oath aloud, or speaks a true name, Dreyfus’s ears perk.

⚠ GM Secret: Dreyfus the Listener

Dreyfus is a shape-walker—Níðhöggr’s eyes and ears in the mortal world. He is Wyrd-bound by guest-right and cannot harm anyone in this room, but he can hear, remember, and report.

For now, let him be an unnerving presence. Do not reveal anything yet. The PCs should feel that the dog is strange, but attribute it to an old animal’s quirks.

Tells (for observant players, hard Intuition test):

  • Dreyfus never blinks.

  • He watches mouths more than hands.

  • He does not eat from the bowl by the hearth (it’s been full for days).

  • When Harald speaks the word "Kaelen," the hound’s ears flatten—not in fear, but in attention.

Part 4: Harald’s Confession

Once the PCs are settled (or at least silent), Harald speaks. Use this as a framework, not a script—let the players interrupt, question, or react.

"The Ever-Ember spoke," Harald begins, his voice flat. "Three weeks ago. It spoke to the Keepers and said the Ever-Embers across Svellheim are dying. That it is tired. That it can no longer hold the boundaries between the living and the dead."

He moves to the table, touches the map. "It promised answers. Promised to explain, after it rested, how we might rekindle the flames. I brought the council together. We prepared. We waited."

He closes his eyes. "The night before the meeting, the Keepers were slain. All seven—every one of the Ember-Keepers in Svellheim. In their beds. No signs of struggle. No witnesses. There are no Keepers left alive."

His jaw tightens. "And the next morning, the dead came for our walls."

Let the PCs process. Then Harald continues:

  • The attacks have escalated: skeletons, ghosts, barrow-wights, all striking the walls at different points.

  • Every defensive plan the council makes is anticipated. The attacks avoid the traps, circumvent the patrols.

  • Harald suspects a traitor—or a leak—within his own command structure.

  • He cannot trust his own court. He cannot trust his own shadows.

Lore: The Ever-Embers and Their Keepers

The Ever-Embers are sanctioned hearth-flames that burn in major settlements across Svellheim. They are not merely fires—they are anchors, points where Yggdrasil’s warmth-flow stabilizes. In folklore, they "keep the dead in their graves" and "hold back the long dark."

The Ember-Keepers (civic office) are chosen every ten years in a sacred rite:

  1. Children of age (13) are paraded past the Ever-Ember during the winter solstice.

  2. The flame itself chooses seven by spitting a coal-ember onto their foreheads, leaving a permanent mark (a scar that glows faintly in darkness).

  3. The chosen seven serve for ten years, then are replaced.

Duties:

  • Maintain the flame’s ring enclosure

  • Manage fuel and ward-lines

  • Raise wind-breaks to protect the flame from storms

  • Speak to the flame on the city’s behalf (a rite performed in silence, with offerings of ash and memory)

The murders are not just political—they are existential. Without Keepers, the Ever-Ember cannot be safely questioned, fed, or calmed. And if the Ever-Ember dies, the dead will flood the city.

Part 5: The Secret Mission

After the PCs absorb the crisis, Harald delivers the mission.

"I called the Konungs-ferð for a reason," Harald says, looking each of you in the eye. "Not for warriors. Not for a war band. I needed people I could trust who no one would suspect. People who owe loyalty to their homes, not to my council."

He straightens. "I need you to find Kaelen. She was one of the seven Keepers. In her second year, she left—abandoned her vows, her mark, everything. No one knows why. No one knows where she went."

"Find her. Bring her back. If anyone can speak to the Ever-Ember now, it’s her."

Let the PCs ask questions. Harald answers honestly (remember: Harald does not lie):

  • Why Kaelen? She was the youngest Keeper ever chosen (age 13, the minimum). She served faithfully for a year, then vanished. Harald believes guilt or fear drove her away—but also believes she still carries the ember-mark and the knowledge required to speak to the flame.

  • What happened to her? No one knows. She left no message. Some think she fled to the Interior Belt. Some think she’s dead.

  • Why us? "Because no one is watching you. Because you’re not on any ledger. Because I can’t trust my own shadows anymore."

  • What about the attacks? "I have soldiers. I have walls. I can hold—for a while. What I don’t have is a Keeper."

  • What if she won’t come? Harald’s jaw tightens. "Convince her. Svellheim’s warmth depends on it."

Part 6: The Interruption — Gragnir

Just as the PCs agree (or object), the shadows move.

"He tells you some of the truth."

The voice comes from the corner, low and rough as ash scraped from stone. A figure steps into the firelight—tall, lean, wrapped in smoke-grey furs. His fingers are black with soot. The smell of burning peat follows him.

Harald’s hand goes to his hip (no sword), then drops. "Gragnir."

The man—Gragnir—nods to the King, then looks at you. "The King wants Kaelen. Good. You’ll need her. But the King doesn’t know where she is. I do. Or rather—I know who does."

Gragnir explains:

  • There is a man named Llewyellen Tosferd—"everyone calls him Lew."

  • Lew has a gift: he can find things that are lost. Through old contracts, favors, and a network of informants that spans the Interior Belt—and through other means he won’t discuss.

  • Lew is currently in Stairwatch, a waystation town just beyond Midgard’s Shield, near the Titan’s Stair pass.

  • It’s a hard trek—ten days through cold, bandit country, and failing routes (~200 miles across the Coastal Belt and Midgard’s Shield)—and the party must travel in secret.

Harald looks grim but doesn’t contradict Gragnir. Instead, he asks: "What’s the price?"

Gragnir smiles, ash crinkling at the corners of his mouth. "For you? Nothing, Highness. For them?" He looks at the PCs. "Lew will name his price when you find him. He always does."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Gragnir’s Manner

Gragnir is not hostile, but he is not comforting either. He speaks like someone who has seen this crisis coming for years and is tired of being ignored.

If the PCs press him:

  • "How did you get in here?" — "The same way the cold gets in. Through the cracks."

  • "Why don’t YOU find Kaelen?" — "Because she’ll run from me. She won’t run from strangers. Not at first."

  • "What aren’t you telling us?" — "Everything I can’t prove yet. When I can, you’ll wish I hadn’t."

  • "Who are you?" — "A man who talks to trees. The trees are dying. That should concern you."

Part 7: The Kicker

As the meeting concludes, Gragnir fades back into the shadows (literally or metaphorically—your choice). Harald gives the PCs what aid he can:

  • Travel papers (forged as "merchants' retinue" to avoid Chainwarden scrutiny)

  • Supplies: 3 days' cold-weather rations, 2 lantern-oil flasks, 1 King’s Cipher Packet (to be used only in dire need)

  • A warning: "Trust no one in my name. If someone quotes my orders, assume they’re lying or compromised."

As you turn to leave, the grey hound—Dreyfus—rises and pads silently to the door. He sits. He watches. And for just a moment, you’d swear the hound smiles.

⚠ GM Secret: The Leak Is Set

Dreyfus has heard everything. The mission. The names. The destination. He will remain at Harald’s side—listening to every war council, every messenger, every whispered order—and relay intelligence through Wyrd-channels: root-whispers carried along Yggdrasil’s dying root-lines. While agents of Grafvitnir may act on this intelligence, Dreyfus serves the deeper entropy.

This is the engine that drives the Grafvitnir Pressure Check for the rest of Act 1. The PCs don’t know they’ve been compromised—but you do.

Narrative consequences for later beats:

  • Grafvitnir’s agents—Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures—know the PCs' mission and destination.

  • Dreyfus staying with Harald means the spy has ongoing access: every update Harald receives about the PCs' progress flows straight to the enemy.

  • The PCs will not learn the source until Beat 8, when Gragnir reveals the truth.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Understood the existential crisis: Ever-Embers dying, Keepers dead, dead rising.

  • Accepted the mission: find Kaelen and bring her back.

  • Met Harald (desperate, honest, out of options) and Gragnir (cryptic, blunt, urgent).

  • Learned the next step: find Lew Tosferd in Stairwatch.

  • Felt the weight of secrecy and paranoia.

  • Been unknowingly compromised—Dreyfus heard every word and remains at Harald’s side, feeding intelligence to Grafvitnir’s network.

Beat 3 — Milestone Exp Summary

No VP-earning encounters this beat. Total: 0 VP.

Transition to Beat 4

The PCs must leave Hrafnborg before dawn, using the forged papers to pass the Chainwardens' outer checkpoints. Harald cannot help them leave—his involvement must remain invisible.

Proceed to Beat 4: The Road to Stairwatch.

Level: 1
Duration: 1–2 sessions
Location: Hrafnborg (departure) → Lantern Road → Frostfen Marshes → Midgard’s Shield → Titan’s Stair → Stairwatch
Key NPCs: Warden-Sergeant Eirik Greyhand (Greypass), Bryn Ketilsdottir (Hollow Waystation)
Type: Two Hard Montage Tests, combat scene, negotiation scene, narrative interludes
Tone: Isolation, hardship, dwindling warmth, the road is the enemy

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 2 | Level: 1 | VP into Level: 2
Beats 4a (Coastal Road) and 4b (Mountain Road) play in sequence - both contribute VP.

Overview

The PCs slip out of Hrafnborg before dawn with forged papers and the King’s supplies. Ahead: two hundred miles of failing roads, frozen marshland, Chainwarden checkpoints, mountain passes, and Fimbulwinter storms—all while maintaining the secrecy of their mission.

This is a long, dangerous journey. Travel rates vary with terrain: 24 miles/day on maintained roads, 18 miles/day through hindered ground (marshes, foothills), and 12 miles/day in severe mountain passes—averaging ~20 miles/day across the full route. The trek takes 8–10 days. The beat is divided into two montage tests separated by encounters, building a rhythm of endurance, danger, and discovery. By the time the PCs reach Stairwatch, they should feel every mile.

Part 1: The Departure

Sólmánuður 6th. Dawn.

You leave Hrafnborg before dawn, slipping through the Chainwardens' outer checkpoints with forged merchants' papers and the taste of salt pork still on your tongues. The Lantern Road stretches north into a world of grey ice and stunted pines.

The King’s instructions are clear: reach Stairwatch. Find Lew Tosferd. Trust no one.

The cold doesn’t care about instructions.

As the PCs pass through the outer checkpoint:

  • The Chainwarden on duty is half-asleep and waves them through with barely a glance. (This changes if the party dawdled or made noise in the King’s halls.)

  • The road beyond the walls is immediately different: the stones are cracked, weeds push through ice, and the mile-markers are buried or missing.

  • The sound of the city fades within minutes. The only sounds are wind, their own breathing, and the crunch of frost under boots.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Leaving Hrafnborg

As the PCs pass through the outer checkpoint, note that Dreyfus is not here—he is back in Harald’s chambers. But every detail of the PCs' mission, destination, and route was spoken aloud in Harald’s sitting room while the hound listened.

Within hours of the PCs' departure, that intelligence begins flowing through Grafvitnir’s Wyrd-channels to agents in the field. The PCs don’t know this yet.

Part 2: Journey Context

Before beginning the first montage test, set the scene with a full description of the route:

Journey: Hrafnborg → Lantern Road north → Frostfen Marshes → Midgard’s Shield foothills → Chainwarden Toll at Greypass → Mountain Road → Titan’s Stair → Stairwatch descent
Distance: ~200 miles
Duration: ~8–10 days through Fimbulwinter terrain
Calendar: Departure Sólmánuður 6th; estimated arrival Sólmánuður 15th–16th
Terrain: Coastal Belt (frozen farmland) → Marshlands → Interior Belt boundary (Midgard’s Shield range)

The Route

  • Days 1–2 — The Lantern Road: Once a well-maintained highway connecting Hrafnborg to the interior towns. Now it’s a ruin: mile-markers buried, road-signs stolen for firewood, sections impassable with snow or debris. Chainwarden patrols tax travellers in Heat (fuel rations) or Word (travel permits). The land is flat, frozen farmland—open sky, cutting wind, and the Ever-Ember’s warmth fading with every mile.

  • Day 3 — The Frostfen Marshes: Where the Lantern Road crosses the old fenlands, the terrain turns treacherous. The marshes haven’t frozen solid—the ice is thin in patches, the reeds hide pockets of black water, and the footcauseway is half-collapsed. The road narrows to a single crumbling path through frozen bog. Something about the Frostfen feels wrong—the cold here is different, wetter, heavier, as if the land itself is exhaling.

  • Days 4–5 — The Refugee Road: Beyond the Frostfen, the Lantern Road meets the Herdweg—the old drovers' highway running east from the Interior Belt. The intersection is choked with refugees fleeing south toward Hrafnborg. Families, merchants, whole villages on the move. The PCs are heading north when everyone else is heading south. Questions will be asked.

  • Days 5–6 — The Chainwarden Toll at Greypass: Where the road enters the foothills of Midgard’s Shield, a fortified checkpoint controls passage. The Toll was built to manage trade; now it manages panic. Chainwardens in cold-bitten armour inspect papers, confiscate contraband, and demand tariffs that grow steeper every month. This is a negotiation scene.

  • Days 6–8 — Midgard’s Shield Foothills: Rocky, wind-scoured ground. The treeline thins. Abandoned crofts and shepherds' shelters dot the landscape—some still have walls, none have roofs. The weather turns from "miserable" to "lethal." The road climbs steadily. The air thins. Storm-fronts roll in without warning.

  • Days 8–9 — The Mountain Road: The ancient road that climbs into the Titan’s Stair proper. Built by giants, if the stories are true—the flagstones are enormous, cracked but enduring. Avalanche debris, rockfalls, and ice-slick switchbacks make the ascent exhausting. A partially collapsed stone bridge spans a gorge halfway up.

  • Day 10 — Titan’s Stair & Descent: The final push: a narrow ice-choked switchback pass through the highest point of Midgard’s Shield. The wind is a physical force. The descent on the far side is steep but windless—a blessed relief—and Stairwatch’s lights are visible from the top.

What the PCs Notice

As they travel, seed these observations:

  • The Ever-Ember’s warmth faded fast once they left Hrafnborg’s radius. The cold deepens with every mile.

  • Roadside shrines to Yggdrasil appear every few miles—small cairns with hollow centres where flame-offerings should sit. Every single one is extinguished. Some have been for weeks.

  • Wildlife is wrong: no birds, no hares, no foxes. The only tracks are old. Something has driven everything south or underground.

  • The people they pass on the road don’t make eye contact. They walk fast, heads down, clutching whatever they can carry. No one stops to talk.

  • The sky doesn’t change colour. Dawn and dusk are the same shade of iron-grey. Only the light’s intensity tells you what time it is.

  • At one point, they pass a farmstead. No smoke from the chimney. The door is open. Snow has drifted inside. (If they investigate: the family left in haste. Food is still on the table—frozen solid.)

Part 3: Montage Test 1 — The Coastal Road

milestones — Type: Montage Test (Coastal Road) | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 2 VP on success, 1 VP partial, 0 VP failure

Covers Days 1–4: Hrafnborg → Lantern Road → Frostfen Marshes → Refugee Road junction.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds (each hero acts once per round; 8 total individual tests).

Rules Reminder:

  • Each hero uses a different skill for each test (no repeating skills across the montage).

  • Skills grant +2 to the test (not an second chance (two rolls, take best)). Edges and banes apply as normal.

  • If a hero has a clever non-test combat action (using an item, ability, or resource), it can earn automatic successes at your discretion.

  • A hero can assist another hero’s test instead of making their own. The assisting hero must use a different skill than the lead. Roll a ability check: tier 1 = second chance (two rolls, take worst) on lead’s test; tier 2 = second chance (two rolls, take best); tier 3 = double second chance (two rolls, take best).

Challenge Characteristic Description

Round 1 Interlude: The Extinguished Shrine

By the roadside, a cairn of stacked stones stands waist-high. Its centre is hollow—a bowl-shaped depression where a flame should burn. The ash is cold. Dead wildflowers, frost-brittle, lie scattered around the base. Someone left offerings. No one came to tend the fire.

The cairn’s keystone is carved with a single runic word. It reads: Remember.

A moment for the PCs to reflect on what the Ever-Embers mean. If anyone makes an offering (even just a breath warmed into their hands and placed in the basin), the cairn’s keystone warms—just for a moment—then goes cold again. Yggdrasil is listening, but too weak to answer.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Find shelter in the Frostfen margins Reason The flat marshland offers no natural shelter. Locate an old peat-cutter’s hut, an overturned barge, or a reed-bank thick enough to break the wind.
Cross the Herdweg junction without attention Presence / Agility The junction is crowded with refugee columns. The PCs need to cross through the flow heading north without drawing questions. Why are young, healthy travellers heading toward the danger?
Resist marsh-sickness Might The Frostfen’s air carries something foul—not disease, exactly, but a heaviness in the lungs. A character who fails wakes the next morning with a rattling cough and bloodshot eyes. (−1 to Might tests until a rest.)
Avoid refugee columns Presence / Agility Families fleeing south toward Hrafnborg clog the road. The PCs need to avoid drawing attention. Questions about their direction—heading north when everyone else flees south—will raise suspicion.
Calm a panicked pack-mule Presence The mule balks at a frozen corpse on the road and threatens to bolt with half the supplies.
Keep morale up at camp Presence The landscape is crushing. Four days of grey-white emptiness and freezing wind. The PCs need to keep each other from despair. Stories, songs, small comforts.

Round 2 Interlude: The Drowned Village

The Lantern Road crosses what was once a shallow lake—you can tell by the reed-stumps and the change in ground texture. At the lake’s centre, barely visible through frost-haze, rooftops jut from the ice. A village, flooded and frozen.

The ice is clear enough to see through in places. Below: a market feet (2 m), complete with stalls and carts. A temple spire, its bell still visible. Streets and doorways and—

Movement. Something shifts below the ice. Not a fish. Something with hands.

The PCs should not investigate. If they do: the ice creaks ominously, and whatever is below tracks their movement across the surface. A easy Reason test identifies this as a draugr nest—the village died and the residents didn’t leave. Breaking the ice would release dozens of them.

If the PCs are wise enough to move on, they gain useful information: the dead are everywhere, not just in obvious battlefields and barrows. The Frostfen is a cemetery that doesn’t know it’s dead.

Montage 1 Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes before failure limit)

  • The party crosses the Frostfen and reaches the Herdweg junction intact.

  • Earn 2 Milestones.

  • Supplies are holding. Morale is manageable.

  • Dreyfus’s intelligence from Hrafnborg hasn’t reached field agents yet—delay the first Grafvitnir Pressure Check by one scene.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • Arrive at the Herdweg junction, but at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Lost a day navigating the Frostfen. One day’s rations consumed for no progress. (−1 day’s supplies.)

    • Health cost: Marsh-sickness. One PC has a persistent cough (−1 to Might tests until a extended rest in a warm building).

    • Remedy available: PCs who forage successfully in the marshes (medium Intuition test) discover marsh-root — the raw ingredient for Frostfen Marsh-Root Poultice. An herbalist PC can prepare one during a rest.

    • Word cost: A refugee recognised the King’s seal on the forged papers and spread the word. The PCs' cover is thinner. (+1 to future Grafvitnir Pressure Checks.)

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party still reaches the junction, but arrives compromised:

    • Lose 2 days' rations and one PC has marsh-sickness.

    • Grafvitnir’s agents know exactly where the party is. The next Pressure Check triggers automatically at 5+.

    • No Milestones earned. Director gains 3 Malice for the next scene.

Montage 1 Failure Scene: Chainwarden Patrol (Optional)

milestones — Type: Combat (Optional - triggered on montage failure) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

This scene triggers on a Total Failure or at the Director's discretion after a Partial Success. A Chainwarden patrol has been tipped off by Ashwright informants or Pale Maw agents. They are waiting at a narrow bottleneck on the Herdweg approach—a stone bridge over a frozen ravine.

The road narrows ahead. A stone bridge spans a frozen creek bed—old stone, Jötunfolk work, still solid after centuries. On the far side, five figures in grey cloaks wait in a loose line. Chain links glint on their tabards. The lead figure raises a gauntleted hand.

“Halt. Toll authority, road inspection. Papers.”

The tone is wrong. This isn't bored routine. They were waiting for you.

If the PCs win the fight:

Part 4: Point of Interest — The Hollow Waystation

Between the first montage and the Chainwarden Toll, the party encounters this location. It can serve as a rest stop or a trap.

The road widens at a stone-built waystation—one of the old Embertender rest-houses that once dotted the major routes. The roof is intact. The walls are solid. Smoke rises from the chimney.

Someone is inside.

Inside: Bryn Ketilsdottir, a former Embertender who abandoned her post when her settlement’s Ever-Ember went out. She’s been living in the waystation for three weeks, burning furniture for warmth, rationing a dwindling supply of dried fish.

  • Bryn is not hostile, but she is deeply ashamed. She failed her oath. The Ember she tended flickered and died in the night—no attack, no catastrophe, it just… stopped. She couldn’t bear to face her community.

  • If the PCs offer compassion: Bryn shares what she knows. The Ever-Embers are going out without external cause. Three more have failed since hers—she heard the news from refugees on the road. "It’s not the cold killing them," she says. "It’s something underneath. The ground is sick."

  • If the PCs push her for information (easy Presence test): Bryn describes the night her Ember died. "I heard a sound. Not from outside—from below. Like something exhaling. The flame bent toward the floor and went out. The stone was warm for an hour afterward, as if the fire had sunk into the earth."

  • If the PCs share food: Bryn is overwhelmed. She insists on giving them her last useful possession—a Chainwarden toll-token (worth one free passage through a checkpoint, saving Heat or Word at the Greypass Toll).

  • If the PCs help Bryn secure the waystation (or if they show genuine kindness), she also offers a battered iron lantern from the waystation’s stores: the Hollow Waystation Lantern. “It was the Embertender’s before mine. The flame won’t die in the wind. Take it — I don’t deserve to carry their light.”

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Bryn

Bryn is not a quest-giver. She is a window into the world. Her story illustrates what Fimbulwinter does to ordinary people—it breaks them not through violence, but through grinding, inexorable failure. If the PCs are kind to her, they earn a toll-token and a contact who may reappear in Act 2 (she can reach Hrafnborg and serve the King). If they ignore her, she’s just another ghost on the road.

Part 5: Negotiation Scene — The Chainwarden Toll at Greypass

milestones — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success

Approximately Sólmánuður 10th. Day 5 of the journey.

Context

Where the Lantern Road enters the foothills of Midgard’s Shield, the Chainwardens maintain a fortified toll crossing called Greypass. It controls the only maintained road through the lower mountains. The toll was built to manage trade caravans; now it manages desperation.

The road narrows between two bluffs of dark stone. A wall of stacked timber and iron bars blocks the path—not a military fortification, but close enough. A gate. A guardhouse. A flag bearing the Chainwarden sigil: a broken chain, re-forged.

Two wardens stand at the gate, wrapped in furs over mail. One holds a ledger. The other holds a spear.

"Papers," says the one with the ledger. It is not a request.

The Scene

This is a Moderate Negotiation with the toll commander, Warden-Sergeant Eirik Greyhand.

Interest Patience
2 3

Motivations:

  • Eirik wants order. He is not corrupt—he is exhausted. The toll is underfunded, understaffed, and overwhelmed. He wants travellers to follow procedure so he can maintain the fiction that the system works.

  • Eirik wants supplies. The toll garrison hasn’t received a resupply in weeks. Heat (fuel, rations) is more valuable to him than coin.

  • Eirik wants to go home. He has a family in Hrafnborg. He hasn’t heard from them in a month. Any news—even bad news—is worth something.

What the PCs Want:

  • Passage through the toll without their cover being blown.

  • Ideally, intelligence about the road ahead (mountain conditions, bandit activity, undead sightings).

  • If they have Bryn’s toll-token, they can present it for automatic free passage for one person. The rest must still negotiate.

Negotiation Beats

Argument Effect
Present the forged papers (Presence) Eirik inspects them. They’re good forgeries. moderate roll+: he’s satisfied. low roll: he notices the seal is slightly wrong—"This isn’t the standard merchant cipher. Where did you get these?" (+1 Interest, as he becomes suspicious but also curious.)
Offer rations or fuel (Heat currency) This speaks directly to Eirik’s need. moderate roll+: earns 2 Interest immediately. Eirik waves them through and offers road intelligence as thanks.
Share news of Hrafnborg (Presence) Tell Eirik the city still stands, the Ever-Ember still burns, and the King is active. moderate roll+: Eirik’s shoulders drop with relief. +1 Interest. He becomes notably friendlier.
Bluff a military connection (Presence) Claim to be on Chainwarden business. high roll only: Eirik buys it and provides an escort for the next 5 miles. low roll–2: Eirik knows every Warden in the district. He doesn’t know you. −1 Patience.
Threaten or intimidate (Might) Catastrophic. Eirik has 8 wardens and a fortified position. −2 Patience. If Patience hits 0 through threats, the PCs are detained and must escape (adding a day to the journey and burning their cover).
Present Bryn’s toll-token Automatic: one party member passes free. Eirik recognises the token and asks after Bryn. If the PCs mention she’s alive and where she is, he is visibly relieved—"She was a good Tender. We all wondered."

Outcomes

Interest Achieved: The PCs pass freely. Eirik provides road intelligence:

"The mountain road is passable but ugly. Two rockfalls in the last week—you’ll need to detour around the second one, near Kross-stead. And watch the gorge bridge—the western footing has shifted. It’ll hold, probably, but don’t linger on it."

He pauses. "There’s also… something else. The patrols up in the high country have been finding tracks. Big ones. Not bear, not wolf. Something that walks on two legs but drags something behind it. We don’t go looking. I’d suggest you don’t either."

If the PCs impressed Eirik during the negotiation (moderate roll+ result), he also offers a confiscated weapon from his evidence locker: the Chainwarden’s Confiscated Dagger. “You’ll need this more than my paperwork does.”

Patience Exhausted: The PCs are treated with suspicion. Choose one: They’re forced to surrender 2 days' rations as "toll payment" (Heat cost). Their descriptions are logged in the ledger. Grafvitnir’s network may access this. (+1 to all future Grafvitnir Pressure Checks.) ** They’re delayed half a day while paperwork is "processed" (Time cost).

Part 1b: Interlude — The Glóðfari’s Caravan

Place this scene immediately after the Greypass negotiation, before the party continues up the mountain road.

Context

In a sheltered hollow just past the checkpoint, a caravan has made camp — five or six wagons in a careful circle, each one patched and reinforced with the wear of many seasons. The canvas is a patchwork of repairs. Between the wagons, people move with the quiet efficiency of a group that has done this a thousand times.

The caravan was also delayed by the Chainwardens. Where the party negotiated their way through, the caravan’s master has been in a quieter, longer standoff. Now that the checkpoint is behind them both, the caravan master notices the party and raises a hand in greeting.

This is Söldís Glóðfari. See Söldís Glóðfari: The Glóðfari’s Arc for her full character document.

A tall woman with copper-brown hair in a practical braid stands by a small fire, tending it with the attention of someone who takes fire personally. Her coat is a patchwork of repairs — each patch from a different settlement, different season. Around her neck, on a cord close to her chest, hangs a sealed clay pot. Even from here you can see the faint glow of something inside — not flame, exactly, but the suggestion of flame. The suggestion of something that has been burning for a very, very long time.

She looks at you — your gear, your pace, your injuries, the way you carry yourselves — and something in her expression says she has already learned more from looking than most people learn from asking.

"You got past Eirik?" She smiles. "He wrote you down in that ledger of his. You’re in the system now. Congratulations."

She gestures toward the fire. "Sit. Eat. You look like people who could use a warm meal more than they could use advice, but I’ll give you both."

The Caravan Members

If the party accepts the meal, they scene Söldís’s caravan family. Use these moments to make the caravan feel real:

Steinþór (mid-30s, the caravan’s muscle) is hauling firewood in loads that should require two people. He nods at the party without speaking, but if anyone offers to help, he accepts with a surprised look — not many people volunteer. He works beside them in companionable silence. If asked about Söldís, all he says is: "She knows where to go. I carry things. It works."

Ragna (70s, herb-woman) is sitting on a wagon seat, wrapped in blankets, and pronounces judgment on the party the moment she sees them: "Thin. All of you. The road is eating you and you haven’t even reached the hard part yet." She produces a small bundle of dried herbs and presses it into the hands of whoever looks most tired. "Brew that tonight. You’ll sleep. You look like people who haven’t slept properly in a week." She’s right.

Einar (10, Ástríðr’s son) approaches the party with the directness of a child who hasn’t yet learned fear: "Are you going to the place where the ice comes from? Óttar says you are. Óttar says you’re going to die. Mama says Óttar doesn’t know what he’s talking about." He looks at Kaelen with particular interest. "Why is your head warm?"

Óttar (early 20s, apprentice trader) hovers nearby, asking questions he’s clearly been told not to ask: "Where exactly are you headed? North, obviously. But where in the north? What’s the actual destination? Because the routes diverge at the Stair, and if you’re taking the eastern fork, I know a man who—" Söldís cuts him off with a look. He retreats, but not far.

Vélaug (35, silent repair-master) says nothing. But when the party finishes the meal, they will notice that a piece of damaged gear — a torn pack strap, a loosened boot heel, a fraying rope — has been quietly repaired. She did it while they ate and never drew attention to it.

What Söldís Offers

During the meal, Söldís answers one direct question the party asks:

  • About the road ahead: What’s actually at Stairwatch, who’s running it, what to expect on the mountain passes.
  • About the Pale Maw: She knows more than is comfortable about their supply lines and checkpoint presence.
  • About the failing settlements: She’s been to most of them. She knows which ones are still holding.

She will not answer a question about the sealed clay pot. "That’s for later," she says, and changes the subject with the ease of someone who has deflected this question a thousand times.

The Critical Moment

Söldís notices Kaelen’s ember-mark. She does not comment on it. She does not react. But she notices — a small change in her expression, not fear, but recognition. She looks at Kaelen a moment longer than is casual, and then looks away.

If Kaelen catches this, all Söldís will say is: "You carry something. I just noticed the shadow it casts."

Mechanical Benefits

  • Ragna’s herbs grant +1 second chance (two rolls, take best) on one Hit Die test during the party’s next rest
  • Vélaug’s repairs restore one piece of damaged gear to full function (no test required)
  • Söldís’s information provides +1 second chance (two rolls, take best) on one knowledge or navigation test in the next beat
  • The warm meal counts as a Hit Die for one party member (Director’s choice or party consensus)

ðŸ“ Director Note: The Caravan as Investment

This scene is designed to make the party care about these people. Ragna’s grumpy kindness, Einar’s fearless curiosity, Vélaug’s silent generosity, Steinþór’s simple reliability — these are individuals, not background props. The party is meeting the people they will eventually protect during the Siege of Hrafnborg.

Every moment of warmth the caravan provides here is a debt the party will feel when Söldís arrives at Hrafnborg’s gates during the siege. These are her people. The party will want to keep them alive.

Part 6: Interlude — The Frozen Messenger

Place this scene on the mountain road between the toll and the second montage.

A figure sits against a roadside boulder, hunched in a cloak. Snow has built up against one shoulder like a small drift. Their skin is blue-grey, their eyes open and glazed with frost.

A leather satchel hangs from one frozen hand.

The body is a courier—a Chainwarden runner, by the badge. Dead perhaps four days. The satchel contains:

  • A wax-sealed letter addressed to "The Watch-Commander, Stairwatch" — the seal is the King’s cipher, but not Harald’s personal seal. Someone else in the court sent this. The letter warns Stairwatch to "detain any travellers matching [the PCs' descriptions] and hold them for questioning."

  • 2 days' rations (frozen solid, but edible if thawed).

  • A small Chainwarden map of the Titan’s Stair pass, annotated with hazard marks. (+1 second chance (two rolls, take best) on one navigation test in the next montage.)

This is evidence that the leak in Hrafnborg is real. Someone besides Harald knows the PCs left. The question: is this the work of a mortal traitor, or has something in Harald’s court been passing information to forces outside the walls?

ðŸ“ GM Notes

Leave this ambiguous. The letter was generated by Dreyfus’s influence—intelligence whispered through Wyrd-channels from the King’s chambers to a compromised court official—but the PCs cannot know that yet. It could equally be a well-meaning functionary acting on incomplete orders.

If the PCs take the letter, they can use it in Stairwatch—either to bluff their way past officials (claiming the courier was delayed and they are the escort) or to warn Lew that Hrafnborg’s security is compromised.

Part 8: Point of Interest — Kross-stead

Place this on the mountain road, roughly Day 7–8.

The road passes through what was once a mining settlement—a dozen stone buildings clustered around a shaft-head, with a crude smelter and a Chainwarden outpost. The outpost flag still flies, but there’s no one here.

The shaft-head is open. Warm air rises from the opening.

Kross-stead was a silver mine. The miners evacuated three weeks ago when the temperature in the lower shafts began rising—slowly, inexplicably. The Chainwarden garrison followed.

  • If the PCs investigate the shaft (not recommended, but PCs are PCs): warm, sulphurous air. The walls of the shaft are veined with something that isn’t silver—a dark, root-like growth that pulses faintly with amber light. This is connected to Yggdrasil’s root system, though the PCs can’t know that yet. Anyone who touches the growth feels a vibration—like a heartbeat, very slow, very deep.

  • If the PCs search the buildings: 1 day’s rations, a miner’s pickaxe (decent weapon), and a Chainwarden logbook. The last entry reads: "The walls of the lower gallery are warm to the touch. Supervisor Alrek says it’s geothermal. Alrek is an idiot. Trees don’t grow underground."

  • If the PCs take rope, pitons, or climbing gear from the mining stores: +1 second chance (two rolls, take best) on one Agility test at Titan’s Stair.

Round 3 Interlude: The Watched Feeling

As you crest a hill, a feeling settles over the group—a prickling at the back of the neck. Not a sound. Not a movement. Just the unmistakable sense that something out there knows where you are.

The ridge to the northwest is empty. The sky is grey. Nothing moves.

But the feeling doesn’t leave.

This is the first effect of Grafvitnir’s network. Intelligence from Hrafnborg has reached agents in the field, and the PCs are now being tracked—not by the hound, but by the Pale Maw’s informant chains: corrupted couriers, compromised Chainwarden posts, and Wyrd-touched carrion birds with silver eyes circling high above.

If anyone uses magic to scan: nothing registers. Grafvitnir’s agents are mortal. The Wyrd-channels that feed them are invisible to arcane detection.

Round 4 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description

Keep the group moving through exhaustion Presence Day 9. Everyone is tired, cold, and running low on supplies. The Stair is the hardest section yet, and moral strength matters as much as physical.
Handle the ice-bridge at the summit Agility / Reason The final crossing before the descent: a natural ice-bridge formed over the top of the pass. It groans underfoot. Characters can test the ice (Reason) to find the strongest path or trust balance (Agility) and move fast.
Descend safely to the Interior Belt Agility The descent from Titan’s Stair is steep and loose—scree, ice-melt channels, and treacherous footing. A controlled descent requires skill; a rushed one invites broken ankles.

Round 4 Interlude: The Cairn Field

Near the summit of Titan’s Stair, the path widens into a flat plateau. Cairns stand here—dozens of them, rough-stacked stone pillars ranging from knee-height to taller than a man. Each one is marked with runes.

These are travel-cairns—monuments left by generations of travellers who survived the crossing. The tradition: add a stone to the pile, speak a name, and the crossing grants safe passage.

Some of the cairns are recent. One is barely a day old—a small stack with a child’s mitten tucked between the stones.

The newest cairn has no runes. Just a word scratched into the topstone with a knife:

HELP.

No mechanical effect, but this scene hammers home the stakes: this road is barely passable even for the desperate, and the PCs are walking it by choice. If any PC adds a stone to the cairn field, the wind drops for exactly one minute—long enough to feel the sun’s warmth through the clouds, faint but real.

Montage 2 Failure Scene: Goblin Ambush (Optional)

milestones — Type: Combat (Optional - triggered on montage failure) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

This scene triggers on a Total Failure or at the Director's discretion after a Partial Success. Goblins—thought extinct in the Titanspine for generations—have emerged from deep mountain caves, driven forward by an unknown power. They are desperate, feral, and organised under a self-styled monarch.

The descent path switchbacks through a narrow defile—loose scree above, a sheer drop below. Ahead, the trail is blocked by a crude barricade of broken cart wheels and piled stones.

Then the chittering starts.

Small shapes scramble along the cliff walls above you. Green-grey skin, yellow eyes, crude weapons. One of them—slightly larger, wearing a battered crown made from bent nails and copper wire—stands on a boulder and shrieks something in a language you don't understand.

It points at you. The others charge.

Goblins. Here. That shouldn't be possible.

What's really happening: These goblins have been driven from their deep caverns by something below—the same power that's raising the dead and pulling things southwest. They don't know what it is. Negotiation difficult but possible: A PC who speaks Caelian or can communicate through gestures can attempt to parley (hard Presence test). Part 11: Arrival at Stairwatch

Approximately Sólmánuður 15th–16th.

Regardless of montage outcomes, the PCs arrive at Stairwatch.

Stairwatch hunches at the foot of Titan’s Stair like a dog that’s been kicked too many times. It’s a waystation town—built for travellers who never stay long, and it shows. The buildings lean. The streets are mud frozen into ridges. Smoke rises from every chimney, but the fires burn low.

The inn-sign reads The Broken Stair. Below it, smaller: Rooms. Ale. No questions.

The town’s Ever-Ember burns in a stone ring at the central crossroads. It’s the smallest you’ve seen—barely a campfire. But it burns. It burns.

What the PCs Find in Stairwatch

  • Population: ~300 permanent residents, plus transient miners, traders, and refugees.

  • Atmosphere: Tense, suspicious, but functional. People here don’t starve—they trade in ore from the Titan’s Stair mines—but they don’t trust outsiders.

  • The Broken Stair Inn: Run by a Dwarf named Hild. She asks no questions, charges fair prices, and waters the ale exactly enough to be annoying.

  • The Chainwarden Post: A small stone building near the Ever-Ember. Two Wardens: one bored, one paranoid. They’ll want to see papers.

  • Lew Tosferd: Not immediately visible. He doesn’t advertise. He lives somewhere in the upper town. Finding him is Easy because he’s well-known around town—but convincing him to help is another matter. (See Beat 5.)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

Roll the Grafvitnir Pressure Check (d6) upon arrival in Stairwatch. This represents Grafvitnir’s network—not Dreyfus himself, who remains in Hrafnborg—acting on relayed intelligence.

Roll Effect
1-2 No network activity. Intelligence from Hrafnborg hasn’t reached local agents yet. The PCs have breathing room.
3-4 A minor complication: a stranger asks too many questions at the inn, or someone follows the PCs to their lodging—a local informant paid by the Pale Maw’s network.
5 A flock of carrion-birds with silver eyes circles the town as the PCs arrive. They circle twice, then fly southwest toward Hrafnborg. Someone is watching from above.
6 Direct interception: the Chainwarden post has received a raven from Hrafnborg with updated descriptions of the PCs. They are detained at the gate for questioning. (This is a roleplay scene—the PCs can talk or bluff their way out.)

Overview

The PCs have arrived in Stairwatch. Their objective is simple: find Lew Tosferd and convince him to locate Kaelen. The problem: Lew doesn’t work for free, he doesn’t trust strangers, and he has his own troubles in Stairwatch that need solving before he’ll consider leaving.

This beat introduces the party’s second major NPC ally, reveals the backstory connection between Lew and Gragnir, and ends with Lew’s finding-gift pointing the way to the Ashen Woods.

Part 1: Stairwatch at a Glance

Stairwatch is not a city. It’s a waystation that forgot to die.

The Town

  • Population: ~300 permanent, plus transients.

  • Economy: Ore from the Titan’s Stair mines, re-sold to traders heading to Hrafnborg or the Interior Belt. Some trapping, some charcoal-burning. Everything else is imported and expensive.

  • Architecture: Stone foundations, timber upper floors, turf roofs. Most buildings lean. Some lean together, forming accidental archways over the narrow streets.

  • The Ever-Ember: Burns in a stone ring at the central crossroads. It’s small—barely a campfire—but it’s warm. Locals gather near it at dusk and dawn. The Keeper here is a frightened teenager named Solvi who was chosen only six months ago.

  • Mood: People here don’t starve, but they don’t sleep well either. The mine produces enough iron to trade, but the miners report strange sounds deep in the tunnels. Three families left last month. Two more are packing.

Key Locations

Location Description
The Broken Stair Inn The only inn. Run by Hild, a Dwarf who asks no questions, charges fair prices, and waters the ale just enough to be annoying. Three rooms upstairs, a common hall with a fire that’s always burning, and a kitchen that smells of smoked mutton.
The Chainwarden Post A small stone building near the Ever-Ember. Two Wardens: Sergeant Tormund (bored, competent, drinks) and Deputy Freja (paranoid, by-the-book, doesn’t drink). They want to see papers.
The Mine Head A gated tunnel entrance at the north end of town. Currently half-operational. Miners go in pairs and don’t work past noon. The deep shafts are sealed—"bad air," they say.
The Upper Town A cluster of older stone buildings on the hill above the crossroads. Better-built, quieter. Lew’s lodging is here—a rented room above a chandler’s shop.
The Bone Market Not bones—the name is old. An open-air market near the gate where trappers sell furs, miners sell raw ore, and everyone trades rumors. Meets every other day.

Part 2: Finding Lew

Lew is not hiding, exactly. He’s just not advertising.

How the PCs Can Find Him

  • Ask at the Broken Stair: Hild knows Lew. "The Finder? Upstairs room, when he bothers to sleep here. More likely at the chandler’s. He owes me three nights' lodging." She’ll point them to the Upper Town.

  • Ask at the Bone Market: Any trader knows Lew. "The one-eyed man? Aye, he found my missing mule last winter. Charged me more than the mule was worth. Try the chandler’s."

  • Ask at the Chainwarden Post: Tormund knows him. "Tosferd? He’s useful. Keeps to himself. Doesn’t cause trouble I have to write up." Freja is more suspicious: "He knows too many people. Nobody knows that many people honestly."

  • Just walk the Upper Town: A man sits on a bench outside the chandler’s shop, whittling a piece of bone. One eye—the left—is real, dark, sharp. The right eye catches the light wrong: it gleams faintly amber, like a coal seen through fog. He watches you approach with the calm patience of someone who’s been expecting you.

First Meeting

"Three strangers with road-dust on their boots and a look like someone’s given them a job they didn’t ask for." The man doesn’t look up from his whittling. The knife moves in small, precise strokes. "Let me guess—someone sent you. Someone important. And they told you I could find something."

He looks up. One eye is dark and human. The other glints amber in the grey light—not glass, not quite stone. Something else.

"Name’s Lew. Sit down. You’re making the chandler nervous."

Lew is friendly, casual, and already three steps ahead. He knows about the Konungs-ferð (common knowledge), suspects these aren’t ordinary travelers (uncommon knowledge), and will listen to their pitch before naming his price.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Playing Lew

Lew is not an obstacle—he’s a transaction. He genuinely wants to help, in the way a merchant genuinely wants to sell you something. He’s warm, funny, and utterly without sentiment about money.

Key behaviors:

  • He asks questions constantly—about the PCs, their homes, their skills. This is both genuine curiosity and professional assessment.

  • He never lies, but he tells you exactly as much as he’s decided to tell you. No more.

  • He refers to his finding-gift obliquely: "I have a knack," "I know people," "things turn up." He does not explain the eye unless pressed far enough.

  • He is never rude. He is always expensive.

Part 3: The Negotiation

milestones — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate (I3/P4) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

This is a Negotiation Scene using Draw Steel’s negotiation rules.

Setup

Interest: 3 (Friendly) — Lew is interested in the job. He just needs proper compensation.
Patience: 4 — He’ll hear them out, but he’s not patient with moralizing or demands.

Motivations (arguments that work):

  • Profit: Offer concrete payment—Heat, Weight, Word, favors, access. "What’s it worth to you?" is music to his ears.

  • Curiosity: Appeal to the mystery. Kaelen is the Keeper who vanished—finding her would be the greatest "lost thing" Lew’s ever tracked. "No one knows where she went. No one." This intrigues him deeply.

  • Self-preservation: The Ever-Embers are dying. If they go out, Stairwatch dies. Lew lives in Stairwatch. This is a hard argument for him to dismiss.

Pitfalls (arguments that backfire):

  • Threats: Lew does not respond to intimidation. He responds by becoming very helpful to your enemies.

  • Moralizing: "You have a duty" or "Think of the people" without concrete context makes his patience drop. He’s heard it all.

  • Demanding charity: "Do this for free because it’s important" is the fastest way to end the conversation. Lew always names his price.

Negotiation Flow

The PCs make arguments. Each argument is a ability check using an appropriate characteristic:

  • Presence for persuasion, flattery, or social pressure

  • Reason for logical arguments, evidence, or strategic framing

  • Intuition for reading Lew and tailoring the pitch to what he actually wants

Refer to Draw Steel’s Negotiation rules for test resolution.

What Lew Wants

If the PCs do well, Lew names his price. He wants two things:

  1. A favor, to be named later. Not a threat—a genuine IOU. Lew trades in favors the way other people trade in iron. "One day, I’ll need something found. Or someone kept unfound. And you’ll help. Fair?"

  2. Help with a local problem before he leaves. Lew can’t just walk out of Stairwatch. He has obligations, debts, and a reputation to maintain. He needs the PCs to handle something first.

Part 4: Lew’s Local Task

Lew has a problem he can’t solve himself because solving it would burn his cover as "just a finder."

Choose one (or both) of the following tasks based on session time and party preferences:

Option A: The Missing Miners

milestones — Type: Combat | Difficulty: Hard (Level 2 Boss) | VP Award: 2 VP on victory

Three miners went into the deep shafts four days ago and haven’t returned. The Mine Head foreman sealed the tunnel and declared "bad air." Lew doesn’t believe it—he thinks something took them. He wants the PCs to go into the mine and bring them back (or bring back proof of what happened).

What’s Down There:

  • The deep shafts broke into an old barrow-complex—pre-Hrafnborg, pre-Svellheim, something older. The miners broke through a wall and found a chamber with runic carvings and a cold that isn’t natural.

  • Two miners are alive, trapped behind a cave-in. One miner is dead—killed by a Barrow-Wight (use Draugr Barrow-Warden stats from Beat 2, or scale up) that guards the old chamber.

  • The runic carvings (medium Reason test) reference "the binding below" and "the wolf that waits." This is a fragment of Grafvitnir lore—the PCs won’t understand it yet, but it connects to the Act 1 climax.

Scene: 1x Barrow-Wight (Level 2 Brute Elite) + 1x Grave-Singer (Level 2 Controller Elite) + 8x Draugr Thrall (Level 1 Brute Minion) — EV 44 (Hard, 0 VP) — plus environmental hazards (unstable ceiling, flooded passages, choking dust). VP scaling: If the party has 8+ VP, a second Barrow-Wight and Grave-Singer emerge from a collapsed side passage at the start of Round 2 (total EV 76, Hard for 10 VP).

Option B: The Debt Collector

milestones — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate (I2/P3) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

A man named Rath has arrived in Stairwatch claiming Lew owes him a significant debt from a previous contract. Rath is technically correct—Lew took payment for a finding-job and then didn’t deliver, because the thing he was hired to find turned out to be a person who was hiding for good reason.

Lew needs the PCs to either:

  • Negotiate with Rath (Moderate Negotiation—Interest 2, Patience 3, Motivations: Justice, Respect; Pitfalls: Lies, Evasion) — convince him to accept alternative payment or forgive the debt.

  • Investigate Rath’s purpose — Rath isn’t just a debt collector. He’s working for someone who wants that hidden person found. If the PCs dig (medium Intuition test, or social investigation), they learn Rath’s employer has connections to the Pale Maw. This task becomes a moral choice: pay the debt (funding enemies) or expose Rath (burning Lew’s reputation).

⚔ï¸ Alternate Reward

If the PCs help Lew with the Debt Collector task, Lew produces a weapon from his personal stash as partial payment: the Chainwarden’s Confiscated Dagger. “Took it off a Warden who owed me a favour. The favour is yours now.” (Only available if the PCs did not already receive it from Eirik in Beat 4.)

Part 5: The Eye

After the PCs complete Lew’s task, he’s satisfied they’re competent and trustworthy enough to travel with. Over a drink at the Broken Stair (or around a campfire if they completed the mine task), Lew explains how he’ll find Kaelen.

Lew reaches up and taps the amber eye—the one that catches the light wrong. He doesn’t remove it, but he turns his head so you can see it clearly.

"Lost the real one years ago. Bad job, bad luck, bad timing—the usual story when something bites you that shouldn’t exist." He shrugs. "Gragnir pulled me out. Stitched me up. And when I was healing, he gave me this."

He taps it again. The amber gleams.

"It’s a gem. Old. He said it was payment for the job—fair trade for an eye, he said." A grim smile. "Took me months to realize what it could do. I was looking for a lost shipment of pelts—routine job—and the eye just… pulled. Like a compass needle. Pointed me right to the cache, buried under a snowdrift twelve miles from where anyone was looking."

He leans back. "Gragnir didn’t know. Or maybe he did and wanted me to figure it out myself. With him, it’s hard to tell."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: The Eye’s Cunning

The gem is genuine—a fragment of something old, possibly connected to Yggdrasil’s sap-amber (fossilized world-tree resin). Gragnir gave it as honest payment, not as a tool. The finding-ability was serendipity: a happy accident of a powerful component in the right host.

The eye works like this:

  • When Lew focuses on something lost (a person, an object, a place that was known and became unknown), the eye warms and pulls in the correct direction.

  • It doesn’t give distances or details—just direction. "That way. Far."

  • It doesn’t work on things that were never known (you can’t find something nobody lost).

  • It doesn’t work on things that are hidden by Wyrd-level power (e.g., Grafvitnir’s prison, the deepest roots of Yggdrasil). It can find Kaelen because Kaelen is hiding by choice, not by cosmic force.

The eye establishes:

  • Lew and Gragnir have history. They’ve worked together before. Lew respects Gragnir but finds him infuriating.

  • Gragnir’s gifts are real but not calculated. He helps people in ways that turn out to be important—but he didn’t necessarily plan for it.

  • Lew’s gift is rare but limited. It’s a compass, not a map. The PCs still have to get to Kaelen.

Lew Uses the Eye

When Lew focuses on Kaelen:

Lew closes his good eye. For a moment, nothing happens. Then the amber eye shifts—not the lid, not the socket, but the light inside the gem. It brightens, dims, brightens again, like a heartbeat.

Lew’s hand comes up slowly and points southeast. Through the wall of the inn, through the mountain, toward the vast grey emptiness of the Interior Belt.

"She’s there," he says. "Past the Shield. Past the dead farms. Deep in the Ashen Woods—what’s left of them." His voice is quiet. "She’s alive. And she doesn’t want to be found."

He opens his eye. The amber fades.

"That’s the Greenweald, friends. Or what used to be it. Three weeks' travel if we’re lucky. More if we’re not." He pauses. "And we won’t be."

Part 6: Level Up

At this point, the PCs have:

  • Survived the road to Hrafnborg (Beat 2)

  • Received the mission from Harald (Beat 3)

  • Crossed Midgard’s Shield to Stairwatch (Beat 4)

  • Completed Lew’s local task (Beat 5)

  • Secured an ally and a heading

The party advances to Level 2.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Level-Up Moment

This is a natural break point. Let the players take a rest, discuss their build choices, and settle into the feeling of having survived the first phase of the journey.

Narratively, frame it as: the PCs have proven they can handle the road, the cold, and the unexpected. They’re not wanderers anymore—they’re agents of something larger, even if they don’t fully understand it yet.

Part 7: Departure

Lew needs one day to settle his affairs in Stairwatch (or less, if the PCs are in a hurry and help him pack). He travels light: a pack, a knife, his whittling bone, and equipment that suggests he’s been on the road more than he’s been home.

Before They Leave

  • Hild the innkeeper packs them extra rations (on the house, if the PCs helped with the mine). "Bring that one-eyed bastard back alive. He owes me."

  • Solvi, the young Ember-Keeper, approaches the PCs at the Ever-Ember. She’s heard they’re heading into the Interior Belt. She presses a coal-ember into a small iron box and gives it to the party: the Solvi’s Ember-Coal Box. “Take this. It’s not much. But… don’t let the fire die. Please.” (This ember can be used once to ignite a campfire that burns warm for 8 hours regardless of weather. Mechanically: automatic success on one fire-related montage test.)

  • A silver-eyed crow perches on the gate post, watching the road southeast. It doesn’t startle when approached — just tilts its head, then lifts away south. (GM note: A Wyrd-touched bird. Grafvitnir’s network uses corrupted carrion birds as spotters. Dreyfus, still at Harald’s side in Hrafnborg, relayed the party’s planned route via Wyrd-channels when he overheard their departure preparations.)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

Roll the Grafvitnir Pressure Check (d6) as the PCs prepare to leave:

Roll Effect
1-2 No activity. Dreyfus’s intelligence hasn’t reached field agents yet — the network is slow this far from Hrafnborg.
3-4 A stranger at the Bone Market asks Hild about the PCs' destination. Hild says nothing, but mentions it to the PCs as a warning. (GM note: A Pale Maw informant acting on intelligence relayed through the network.)
5 The Chainwarden post receives a raven the morning the PCs plan to leave. Deputy Freja comes to the inn asking pointed questions. The PCs must talk fast or leave immediately. (GM note: A corrupted official responding to network directives.)
6 Rath (from Option B, or a different agent if you used Option A) is found dead in his room. A rune is carved into his palm: the mark of the Pale Maw. Stairwatch goes into lockdown. The PCs must escape town (Easy montage, 2 rounds) or wait a day and miss their window.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Found and allied with Lew Tosferd.

  • Completed a local task that established their competence and moral compass.

  • Learned that Lew and Gragnir have a shared history (the eye, the old job, the injury).

  • Learned Kaelen’s direction: deep in the Ashen Woods (the Greenweald).

  • Advanced to Level 2.

  • Felt Grafvitnir’s network tightening (suspicious strangers, pointed questions, silver-eyed birds, a sense of being watched).
