Overview

The longest leg of Act 1. The PCs travel southeast from Stairwatch, across the failing Interior Belt, and ultimately to the Green Heart—the last surviving grove of the Greenweald.

Distance: ~160 miles via the Ashen Woods (primary route), or ~125 miles via the Bone Fields and Frozen Steppe (alternate route)
Duration: ~9 days (Ashen Woods) or ~7 days (Bone Fields) — mildly hindered terrain throughout (18 miles/day)
Calendar: Departure approximately Sólmánuður 20th; arrival approximately Sólmánuður 28th–29th

The journey is broken into three montage test rounds separated by combat encounters, narrative interludes, and points of interest. By the time the PCs reach the Green Heart, they should understand what the world has lost—and what, impossibly, still clings to life.

Part 1: The Route Choice

Before departure from Stairwatch, Lew presents the options. This is a meaningful player decision.

Lew spreads a stained map across the table at the Broken Stair. His amber eye glows faintly—brighter when his finger traces southeast.

"Two ways to get where we’re going," he says, tapping the map. "Neither is good."

He draws a long curve south. "Through the Ashen Woods. What’s left of the Greenweald. Hundred and sixty miles of charcoal and silence. Slow going—the roads are gone, the ground is treacherous, and the things living in the char don’t like visitors." He pauses. "But there’s cover. Shelter. And the woods are… connected to where we’re going. We’ll find the trail."

His finger shifts east, cutting a straighter line across open ground. "Or through the Bone Fields. Hundred and twenty-five miles—shorter, faster. But the Fields are open country. No cover, no shelter, and the cold is worse than anything you’ve felt. There’s a reason they call it the Frozen Steppe on the far side." He taps his amber eye. "I’ve been through the Fields before. With Gragnir. It didn’t go well."

He looks at you.

"Your call."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Route Choice

Both routes lead to the Green Heart. The choice affects the character of the journey, not the destination.

Ashen Woods (160 miles, ~9 days):

  • Longer but with more shelter and cover.

  • The PCs travel through devastated forest—oppressive, haunted, but navigable.

  • Grafvitnir’s network has a harder time tracking them (the dead woods confuse the Wyrd-touched carrion birds and informant chains).

  • More biological threats: blighted beasts, poisoned water, corrupted root-things.

  • Lew’s eye pulls them unerringly through the pathless interior.

  • Better connection to the Green Heart’s story—they see what the world lost.

Bone Fields / Frozen Steppe (125 miles, ~7 days):

  • Shorter but brutally exposed.

  • The Bone Fields are an ancient killing ground—bones of men and giants jut from the frozen earth. The Steppe beyond is flat, featureless tundra.

  • Grafvitnir’s network can observe them easily on open ground (+1 to all Grafvitnir Pressure Checks on this route).

  • More undead threats: the Fields are rich with restless dead. The Steppe’s cold tests endurance.

  • Lew has personal history here (opens his backstory about Gragnir earlier).

  • Greater tactical risk but faster arrival—important if the PCs suspect a time pressure.

If the players can’t decide, Lew recommends the Ashen Woods: "I know the Fields. That’s exactly why I don’t want to go back."

The Ashen Woods route is detailed as the primary path below. The Bone Fields route follows as an alternate path.

After completing your chosen route (Beat 6a or Beat 6b), the following applies to both routes.

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

Roll the Grafvitnir Pressure Check (d6) at the end of this beat:

Roll Effect
1-2 No network activity. The Ashen Woods disrupted Wyrd-channel tracking, or the Bone Fields' restless dead interfered with the informant chains. Grafvitnir’s field agents have fallen behind.
3-4 The PCs find tracks near their campsite: booted feet, not animal. Someone circled the camp and left heading south. A Pale Maw informant has pinpointed their position and reported it through the network.
5 A flock of Valravn — carrion-birds with silver eyes — circles above the party for an hour. They don’t attack, but they’re clearly observing. Grafvitnir doesn’t need agents on the ground when his master’s eyes are in the sky.
6 Direct scene: a Pale Maw agent (Level 2 human cultist, Shadow-type) attempts to ambush the party at camp. The agent carries a message carved in bone: "The Wolf remembers. The tree forgets." If captured, the agent knows nothing useful — they’re a zealot, not a strategist. They received their orders through the network and have never met Dreyfus or Grafvitnir.

Lew Character Development

During the long journey, Lew opens up—not all at once, but in small moments. Seed these conversations into rest stops and quiet travel:

  • About Gragnir: "We worked together years ago. A job in the Bone-Fields—don’t ask. He saved my life. I lost my eye. He gave me the gem as payment. 'Fair trade,' he said." A pause. "I’m still not sure he was wrong."

  • About the finding-gift: "It’s not magic. Or if it is, it’s the kind of magic that doesn’t know it’s magic. The eye just… remembers where things were, and notices when they’re not there anymore. Like an itch you can’t scratch until you find what’s missing."

  • About Kaelen: "Never met her. But I’ve heard the name. Gragnir mentioned her once—said she was 'the best and worst thing to happen to Hrafnborg.' Wouldn’t explain what he meant."

  • About himself: "I don’t have a home. I have rooms I sleep in more than others. Stairwatch is the closest thing, and that’s only because Hild hasn’t kicked me out yet." A smile. "I find things for people. That’s what I do. It’s not heroic. But it’s useful."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Lew’s Backstory Pacing

If the party took the Bone Fields route, Lew’s full Gragnir story has already been told (see the Bone Fields interlude above). In that case, use these conversation beats to fill in the other details—his finding-gift, his knowledge of Kaelen, his sense of himself.

If the party took the Ashen Woods route, these are the primary opportunities for Lew’s backstory. The Bone Fields story can be told in abbreviated form around a campfire: "I went there once with Gragnir. Lost my eye. Got this instead." He doesn’t elaborate unless pushed.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Crossed the Interior Belt and reached the Green Heart via the Ashen Woods or the Bone Fields.

  • Experienced the devastation of the burned / frozen world firsthand.

  • Survived combats appropriate to their route (Rot-Beasts + Rootgnaw, or Barrow-Draugr + Rootgnaw).

  • Discovered elements of the living root network that connects the Green Heart to Yggdrasil.

  • Met people surviving in the wasteland (Solveig in the Ashen Woods; or faced the deeper loneliness of the Bone Fields).

  • Bonded with Lew through shared hardship and conversation.

  • Arrived at the boundary of the Green Heart with a heading on Kaelen.

  • Felt Grafvitnir’s network tighten or temporarily loosen.

Part 2: Setting the Scene

The Dead Farms

South of Stairwatch, the world opens up. The mountains fall behind you, and the Interior Belt unfolds: flat grey fields that should be farmland, dotted with crofts that have gone dark. Fences still stand, but nothing grows behind them. Silos stand open and empty. Animal pens hold only snow.

Lew walks ahead, his amber eye half-closed, one hand gesturing southeast like a man following a sound only he can hear.

"She’s still there," he says, every morning and every evening. "Still alive. Still hiding."

The road—what’s left of it—leads into the grey.

The Ashen Woods

The first sign is the smell: charcoal and wet ash, as if the fire that burned here never quite stopped smouldering. Then the trees begin.

They aren’t trees anymore. They’re pillars of charcoal—sixty, seventy feet tall, stripped of bark and branch, standing in rows that go on forever. The Greenweald was a forest. Now it’s a graveyard of wood.

Nothing moves. Nothing calls. The only sound is the crunch of char under your boots and the occasional crack of a dead trunk giving way to its own weight.

The sky is grey. The ground is grey. The trees are black.

And deep in the distance, Lew’s eye pulls you on.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: The Ashen Woods Atmosphere

The Ashen Woods are not dangerous because of obvious monsters. They’re dangerous because they’re wrong. The War of Fire (Year 2 of Fimbulwinter) burned the Greenweald deliberately—to deny cover to enemies and produce fuel. What’s left is a landscape that shouldn’t exist: a forest-shaped absence.

Key sensory details to deploy:

  • Silence. No birds, no insects, no wind through leaves. The silence is aggressive—it pushes back against conversation.

  • Charcoal dust. Everything is covered in a fine black powder. It gets in clothes, lungs, eyes. By the end of day one, the PCs are grey-black from head to foot.

  • Scale. The forest was enormous. The dead trunks go on for miles in every direction. It takes days to cross. The monotony is its own hazard.

  • The occasional green. Every few miles, a small patch of moss or a single green shoot pushes through the char. These are the woods trying to come back. They’re failing, but they’re trying.

Round 1 Interlude: The Last Farm

Between Round 1 and the combat scene, stage this brief scene:

The last farmstead before the Ashen Woods is larger than the others. A proper longhouse, stone-built, with a turf roof that’s still mostly intact. The door is barred from the outside.

Inside: a family’s belongings, neatly packed. A note on the table, weighted with a stone. The handwriting is shaky:

"We go southwest. May the Ember at Hrafnborg still burn. If you read this and the fire is out—God help you. God help us all."

The Ever-Ember niche in the wall is cold. The ash is weeks old.

Let the PCs react. If they search the house: preserved food (1 day’s rations), a good hand-axe, and a child’s doll left behind on a bed. The doll’s face is turned toward the south—toward the woods—as if watching something.

Part 4: combat scene — Rot-Beasts at the Treeline

milestones — Type: Combat (Ashen Woods) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

As the PCs leave the farms and enter the Ashen Woods, the first real threats emerge.

Context

The Ashen Woods' charcoal shell hides something worse than dead trees. The burning didn’t kill everything—some things changed. Animals warped by the blight that followed the fire: rot-touched, aggressive, territorial. They don’t attack for food. They attack because their territory has been violated.

The Scene

Setting: The treeline—the border between open farmland and the burnt forest. Charcoal trunks rise like prison bars. The ground is soft with ash and hidden roots.

Enemies:

  • 7 Rot-Beasts (Level 2 Brute Minion)

  • 2 Vine Stranglers (Level 2 Ambusher Elite)

Characteristics: Might +3, Agility +1, Reason −3, Intuition +1, Presence −2

Features:

  • Charge: When the Rot-Beast moves 3+ feet in a straight line before a strike, the strike deals +5 damage.

  • Blighted Hide: Any creature that makes a melee Free Attack against the Rot-Beast takes 3 corruption damage (thorns, spines, and fungal barbs).

Abilities:

  • Gore (Combat Action, Melee 1, Might): 4/7/10 + Might damage. On tier 3, the target is pushed 2 feet and falls prone.

The Frenzy trigger is their defining moment; signal it dramatically ("The beast screams—a sound like tearing wood—and its eyes blaze green").

Aftermath

The beasts lie still—or what’s left of them. Up close, they barely look like animals. The boars' tusks are wood. Their hides are patchy with bark and fungus. The vine-thing dissolves into the ash, already rotting.

Lew looks at the remains and says nothing for a long time.

"The woods are dying," he says finally. "Everything in them is dying. But dying things fight hardest."

If the PCs investigate the remains (easy Reason test): the blight is organic, not magical. Something in the soil—in the root system—is corrupt. The trees burned, but the roots didn’t. Whatever lives in those roots is spreading poison through the ground.

Part 5: Montage Test — Round 2: Deep in the Ashen Woods (Days 4–6)

After the combat, the montage continues. The woods close in. The silence deepens.

Round 2 Interlude: The Green Thread

Midway through the deep woods, Lew stops.

"Shh." Lew holds up a hand. His amber eye is open, bright, almost pulsing.

He kneels and brushes ash away from the ground. Beneath the char—green. A single root, thick as a man’s arm, running through the dead soil. It’s alive. Pale green bark, sap beading at the surface, warm to the touch.

Lew follows it with his fingers. It runs southeast—the same direction the eye has been pulling. Toward the centre of the woods.

"That’s not a tree root," he says quietly. "That’s a taproot. Something at the centre of these woods is still alive. And it’s been feeding the whole forest from below, trying to bring it back."

He looks up at you. "That’s where she is."

This root is a tendril of the Green Heart—the surviving grove. It is old, deep, and connected ultimately to Yggdrasil’s root network (though the PCs won’t know this yet). Kaelen lives at the centre of this network.

Part 6: Point of Interest — The Charcoal Cathedral

Place this between Round 2 and Round 3, as the PCs push deeper into the Ashen Woods.

The trees change. Not in colour—still black, still dead—but in arrangement. The trunks here grow closer together, and their upper branches interlock overhead, forming a natural vault. The charcoal ceiling is fifty feet up. Ash motes drift downward like black snow.

It looks like a cathedral. A vast nave of dead wood, pillared and vaulted, stretching southeast into darkness.

And on the "altar"—a flat slab of stone in the clearing at the cathedral’s centre—someone has left an offering: a bowl of still-warm porridge, a clay cup of water, and a sprig of green holly.

Someone is tending this place. Someone who is alive, nearby, and careful.

If the PCs wait: nothing happens. The offering is left by a charcoal-burner—one of the last humans living in the Ashen Woods. She will not show herself unless the PCs leave an offering in return.

If the PCs leave food, a tool, or a kind word:

You hear a sound you haven’t heard in the woods: a human throat clearing.

A woman emerges from behind a pillar. She is thin—terribly thin—with skin stained grey-black by years of charcoal dust. Her clothes are ash-coloured furs. She carries a handaxe and a leather satchel.

"You’re heading for the green place," she says. It is not a question.

This is Solveig the Burner—a former woodcutter who refused to leave when the forest was put to the torch. She has lived in the Ashen Woods for three years, burning charcoal for fuel, navigating by the root-patterns beneath the ash, and avoiding the blighted beasts.

  • What Solveig knows: She’s seen the Green Heart from a distance—a "wall of thorns and light." She’s also seen the root-system growing beneath the ash. "The ground is alive under the dead. The roots glow at night—amber, like your friend’s eye." She glances at Lew.

  • What Solveig wants: News. She hasn’t spoken to another person in four months. She’ll talk for hours if they let her. She also wants a knife—hers is worn to a nub.

  • What Solveig offers: If treated well, she shares a shortcut through the cathedral that saves half a day’s travel (+1 second chance (two rolls, take best) on one test in Round 3). She also warns them about the Rootgnaw Brood: "There are things that chew the green roots. Big as dogs. Bigger. They come from the deep ash, and they’re getting bolder."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Solveig

Solveig is a survivor—not a quest-giver, not a tragic victim, but a stubborn, practical human who decided the Ashen Woods are her home now. She represents the world’s resilience. If the PCs are kind, she becomes a contact in the woods who may reappear in Act 2 (guiding allies through the Ashen Woods). If they ignore her, the offering goes cold, and the cathedral feels lonelier.

If the PCs harm her or take her supplies: she vanishes into the ash. She knows these woods better than anyone alive. They will not find her.

Part 7: Post-Montage Interlude — The Singing Stone

The path opens into a clearing—one of the few spaces in the Ashen Woods where you can see the sky. At the clearing’s centre stands a stone—not a cairn, not a marker, but a single upright boulder of dark granite, roughly cylindrical, taller than a man.

It’s humming.

Not audibly—you feel it in your teeth, your fingertips, the soles of your boots. A vibration rising from the ground, channelled through the stone, and radiating outward. The ash on the ground around the stone has been pushed aside by the vibration, revealing bare earth—and roots. Dozens of roots, all converging on the stone like spokes on a wheel.

Lew presses his palm against the stone. His eye flares bright amber.

"The Tree," he whispers. "This stone is sitting on one of the Tree’s roots. A deep one. I can feel it all the way down. Miles down."

This is a root-node—a point where Yggdrasil’s deep root network surfaces. The stone was placed here by the original Ember-Keepers as a marker. The PCs don’t know this yet, but in Act 2 these nodes become waypoints for their journey to the Emberwell.

If any PC touches the stone: they feel warmth—genuine, deep warmth, like standing beside a great fire. For one moment, the Fimbulwinter cold lifts entirely. Then it returns, and the warmth fades, and the world is grey again.

If Kaelen were here, she would weep. The stone is still connected. The root is still alive.

  • Earn 2 Milestones.

  • The party has supplies, composure, and time to prepare before entering the grove.

  • Grafvitnir’s network is behind them — the dead woods disrupted Wyrd-channel tracking and the field agents lost the trail.

    • Morale cost: The Ashen Woods have worn on the party. One PC is rattled (−2 to Presence tests until a extended rest).

Part 9: combat scene — The Rootgnaw Brood

Near the boundary of the Green Heart, where living roots push up through dead soil and patches of impossible green moss glow in the charcoal twilight, the PCs scene the Rootgnaw Brood—creatures that feed on the Green Heart’s living root system.

Context

These aren’t natural animals. They’re blight-spawn—things born from the corruption in the Ashen Woods' soil that are drawn to the Green Heart’s life the way moths are drawn to flame. They’re trying to eat their way to the source. The dryads of the Green Heart have been fighting them for years.

The Scene

Setting: A clearing where living roots have broken through the charcoal crust. Green moss covers the ground. The air smells of sap and rot simultaneously. Three large roots—each as thick as a barrel—pulse with amber light.

Enemies:

  • 2 Rootgnaw Queens (Level 3 Controller Platoon)

  • 1 Vine Strangler (Level 2 Ambusher Elite)

Birdsong.

Lew points. His eye is burning bright now, almost uncomfortable to look at. "She’s close. Maybe a day’s travel. Maybe less."

Beat 6b — The Bone Fields

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Running the Alternate Route

If the PCs choose the Bone Fields, use this section instead of Parts 2–8 above. The beat still ends at the Green Heart (Part 9: Rootgnaw Brood continues as normal regardless of route taken), but the character of the journey is completely different.

The Bone Fields route is shorter but harsher: open terrain, more undead, worse cold, and Grafvitnir’s network can observe them easily. It also unlocks Lew’s backstory earlier—he has history here.

The Bone Fields: Setting

East of Stairwatch, the mountains give way to broken flatland—a vast, wind-scoured basin where nothing grows and the wind never stops. The Bone Fields.

The name is literal. Bones jut from the frozen earth: ribs the size of longships, femurs as tall as pine trees, skulls half-buried with eye sockets wide enough to camp in. These are the bones of giants—or something older—fallen in a war so ancient that even the skalds have forgotten which side won.

Between the bones: graves. Thousands of them. Stone-ringed barrows, a day’s march in every direction, their capstones cracked and frost-heaved and—in too many cases—open.

Lew stands at the edge of the Fields and goes very still.

"Last time I was here," he says quietly, "the ground split open and something came out of the earth. Something old." He touches his amber eye. "That’s where this came from."

He takes a breath. "Stay close. Don’t touch the bones. And for the love of all that’s warm, don’t open any barrows."

Round 1 Interlude: Lew’s Story

By the campfire, sheltered in the lee of a giant’s shoulder blade:

Lew stares into the fire. The amber eye reflects the flame strangely—the light bends toward it, as if the eye is drinking warmth.

"Gragnir brought me here three years ago. A job—finding something he’d lost. A seed, he said. A seed the size of my fist, wrapped in leather. He’d left it here 'for safekeeping.'" Lew laughs bitterly. "'Safekeeping.' In the Bone Fields."

He taps his bad eye. "We found the seed. We also found something else. The ground cracked open beneath us—not an earthquake, not a cave-in, something deliberate. Like the earth was opening its mouth. And out of the crack came… I don’t know what it was. Big. Old. Made of roots and bone and frozen soil. It looked at Gragnir and spoke."

A long pause.

"Gragnir spoke back. In a language I don’t know—something that made my teeth ache and my eye bleed. The thing listened. Then it went back into the earth, and the crack closed, and Gragnir was holding the seed and my eye was gone."

He touches the amber gem.

"He put this in the socket himself. Said it was 'fair exchange for what the earth took.' I didn’t understand then." He looks at you. "I still don’t. But I can feel the green thread, and I can find what’s lost, and my dead eye sees more than the living one ever did."

This is the fullest telling of Lew’s backstory the PCs will get in Act 1. It establishes:

  • Gragnir’s deep connection to the earth and its inhabitants.

  • The Bone Fields sit atop something ancient and aware—part of Yggdrasil’s root network.

  • Lew’s eye-gem is literally part of the tree. It was given in exchange for a human eye, making Lew a living bridge between the mortal world and the deep roots.

He stands. "The Fields go deep. Deeper than bone. Let’s not be here when something deeper decides to wake up."

Round 2: The Frozen Steppe (Days 4–7)

Beyond the Bone Fields, the terrain flattens into featureless tundra—the Frozen Steppe. The party must cross this to reach the Green Heart from the east.

Round 2 Interlude: The Sky Changes

Lew stops. He’s been stopping more often—his eye burns, his head aches, and twice today he’s turned in a slow circle and pointed in a slightly different direction, correcting something only he can feel.

But now he’s not correcting. He’s staring south.

"Look at the sky," he says.

You look. It takes a moment to see it. The sky over the Steppe is grey—the same grey it’s been for a week. But to the south, near the horizon, the grey is… different. Darker. Richer. Not storm-clouds—something organic. Something that absorbs light rather than blocking it.

"That’s the canopy," Lew says. His voice is awed. "The trees. The real trees. The Heart is still growing."

He points. "Two days. Maybe less."

The party has spotted the Green Heart’s second chance (two rolls, take best) from the east. They still need to navigate to it, but they now have a visual bearing. The Heart appears as a dark smudge on the horizon—the only thing taller than grass-height for fifty miles.

Overview

The PCs enter the Green Heart—the impossible: a living forest at the center of the dead Ashen Woods. Here, Kaelen has hidden for years, tending the grove and its furious dryad guardians. She doesn’t want to be found. She doesn’t want to go back. She believes she caused the crisis, and she’s terrified of making it worse.

This beat is the emotional center of Act 1. The PCs must convince Kaelen to return to Hrafnborg, and the outcome of the negotiation determines how she joins—as a willing ally or a reluctant one forced by circumstance.

Part 1: Entering the Green Heart

The Boundary

The change happens over the course of a mile—and then all at once. The charcoal pillars thin. Ash gives way to soil—real soil, dark and damp. A single green fern uncurls from the ground, then another, then dozens, then hundreds, and suddenly the world is alive.

Trees. Real trees. Birch and ash and oak, winter-bare but breathing—bark intact, roots gripping living earth, branches reaching for a sky that seems somehow less grey here. Snow dusts the ground, but beneath it: moss, roots, the smell of wet earth and growing things.

A wall of thorns—five feet thick, eight feet tall—blocks the path forward. Living bramble, dense as castle walls. Within the thorns, you can hear birdsong. You can feel warmth. Not fire-warmth. Growing-warmth.

Something moves in the canopy above you. A face—bark-skinned, hollow-eyed, beautiful and terrible—watches you from the leaves and says nothing.

The Dryad Welcome

The dryads of the Green Heart are protectors, not friends. There are three of them (Level 4 Controller Standards if combat occurs), and they have been defending this place since the War of Fire with murderous dedication.

Their default response to intruders: Kill them. Quietly. Efficiently. Feed the bodies to the roots.

Why the PCs aren’t dead: Two reasons.

  1. Lew’s eye. The amber gem resonates with the root network. The dryads sense it—it feels like Yggdrasil to them, and they hesitate.

  2. Kaelen. She’s been watching (through the trees, through the roots, through the dryads' eyes) since the PCs crossed the Rootgnaw battleground. She told the dryads to let them through.

The thorn-wall shifts. Not opens—shifts. The brambles pull apart like fingers unclenching, leaving a gap just wide enough for one person at a time.

A voice drifts from deeper in the grove: "Come in. Leave weapons sheathed. The trees are watching."

If the PCs hesitate, the dryad in the canopy hisses: "The Keeper said come. So come. Before we change our minds."

Part 2: The Green Heart

The grove is warm. Not Hrafnborg-warm—not the press of bodies and braziers and desperation—but earth-warm. The warmth rises from below, from the roots that pulse with amber light. The air smells of resin and rain.

A clearing opens at the center: perhaps a hundred feet across, ringed by ancient oaks. Their canopies interlock overhead, forming a living ceiling. Moss carpets the ground. A stream—clear, unfrozen, impossible—runs through the clearing’s center.

Near the stream, a young woman sits cross-legged on a stone. She’s wearing practical traveling clothes—patched, repaired, forest-stained. Her hair is dark, cropped short. On her forehead, a scar glows faintly amber in the grove’s light: the ember-mark.

She doesn’t look up as you enter. She’s carving something into a piece of bark with a small knife. Her hands are steady. Her jaw is tight.

"I know why you’re here," she says. "The answer is no."

This is Kaelen—the Seventh.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Playing Kaelen

Kaelen is not a damsel to be rescued. She is not waiting to be convinced. She has made a deliberate, reasoned, guilt-driven choice to stay here, and she will defend that choice with intelligence and conviction.

Key behaviors:

  • She speaks directly—no evasion, no deflection. She will tell the PCs exactly why she won’t go.

  • She is not hostile. She is tired. She has been carrying this guilt alone for years.

  • She genuinely cares about Svellheim’s people. That’s what makes this hard.

  • She is terrified—not of the mission, but of herself. She believes she broke something fundamental, and she fears she’ll break it worse.

  • She is protective of the Green Heart and its dryads. They are her family now.

Part 3: Kaelen’s Story

Before the formal negotiation begins, Kaelen explains herself. This is both exposition and a chance for the PCs to understand what they’re asking of her.

What Kaelen tells them willingly:

  • She was chosen as Ember-Keeper at thirteen. Youngest ever. She served faithfully for three years.

  • During a duty journey in her third year, she came upon a northern village in crisis—the entire population sick, fires unattended, children and families huddled freezing in the temple. She used the town’s Ever-Ember to start hearth fires, heat water, and cook food.

  • She broke the prohibition she knew by heart: “The Ever-Ember gives warmth, but does not share its fire.” An Ember may warm those who sit by it, but flame may not be taken from it to light other fires. The Ember’s power is its singularity—an unbroken anchor between the living and the dead.

  • There was a small, real consequence—a slight dimming of the anchor for a season, a few more restless dead in that region that winter. Nothing provable. Nothing anyone could trace to her. But Kaelen noticed. She counted it.

  • When Fimbulwinter came and the dead rose everywhere, she added her small real effect to the account and inflated it into the cause. She believes her single act of mercy cracked the wall between the living and the dead. The guilt has been her only companion for twelve years.

  • She came here because the Green Heart is the only place left that feels whole. The dryads don’t ask what she did. They care about the grove. She tends the roots. They protect the boundary. It works.

GM Notes: Playing the Guilt Scene

This confession should feel honest, not theatrical. Kaelen is not performing her guilt. She has lived with it, examined it from every angle, and concluded it is real and deserved. She does not flinch from it.

The critical thing the PCs must understand: she is not wrong that she did something that had a real effect. She lit fires from the Ember. The anchor dimmed. People did rise that winter. Her guilt is founded on something true. What she got wrong is the scale—she assigned civilization-ending weight to a small real harm, because that is what guilt does when it has no one to correct it.

Do not let PCs dismiss her guilt. The pitfall rules below exist precisely to prevent this. Her guilt is the load-bearing wall of her identity right now.

What Kaelen does NOT tell them (GM secret—Layer 2, Act 3 reveal):

The Ever-Ember spoke to Kaelen directly, before she fled—something it had never done with any Keeper before and left no record of. It showed her prophetic visions: Fimbulwinter, the War of Fire, the dead rising everywhere. All true. All fulfilled.

Then it showed her something she has never admitted to anyone: the other six Keepers, years later, placing a carved stone in her empty seat to replace her role in the witnessing rite. She saw this and read it as her abandonment and erasure—the other Keepers erasing her from the record while the world ended without her. She left to spare herself from being forgotten. She didn’t understand she was seeing their manipulation by Grafvitnir’s agent.

What the Ember was actually showing her: the void her departure would leave, which Grafvitnir’s agent would exploit to void the binding. Yggdrasil showed her this vision deliberately—not to warn her, but to move her. The tree needed her gone before the corruption of the other Keepers made her complicit. Her departure was guided, not accidental.

The thing that broke her was not the vision of apocalypse. It was the vision of being replaced by a rock. That is the secret she carries that no one knows. (This is revealed in Act 3, when the party learns what the other Keepers actually did and why.)

Part 4: The Negotiation

This is a Very Hard Negotiation Scene.

The rules-reference defines Very Hard as I1—2 / P2. Kaelen’s starting Interest of 2 (rather than 1) might suggest Hard difficulty, but the label is warranted because: (1) pitfalls cost both Interest and Patience (—1/—1), making a single misstep almost certainly fatal to the negotiation; (2) non-motivation arguments penalise Interest on T1; (3) the effective target is Interest 4 (not the standard 5), but Patience 2 means only two arguments total—no room for hit die; (4) each motivation can only be invoked once.

Setup

Interest: 2 (Suspicious) — Kaelen cares about Svellheim. She doesn’t want people to die. But she’s made a deliberate, reasoned choice to stay, and she will defend it.
Patience: 2 — She’s already decided. The PCs have very little room to movement combat action. Wasted arguments end this conversation.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Draw Steel Negotiation Mechanics

Under Draw Steel rules, each argument is a ability check using Reason, Intuition, or Presence (with any applicable interpersonal skill). Motivations require a medium difficulty roll; arguments with no motivation or pitfall require a difficult roll.

Motivation appeal (medium difficulty):

  • low roll (11 or lower): Patience -1. No Interest change.

  • moderate roll (12—16): Interest +1, Patience -1.

  • high roll (17+): Interest +1, Patience unchanged.

No motivation or pitfall (difficult):

  • low roll: Patience -1, Interest -1.

  • moderate roll: Patience -1 only.

  • high roll: Interest +1, Patience -1. (Natural 19—20: Patience unchanged.)

Pitfall invoked: Automatic failure. Interest -1, Patience -1.

With Patience 2 and Interest 2, the party needs to reach Interest 4 for Kaelen to agree (“Yes—I’ll come, on my terms”). Two successful high roll motivation appeals can do it cleanly. One moderate roll hit and one high roll hit spends all Patience. One pitfall invoked almost certainly ends the negotiation in failure. Stakes are real.

Each motivation can only be successfully invoked once.

Motivations (medium difficulty roll — arguments that can raise Interest):

  • Redemption as combat action, not absolution: Do NOT frame this as “fix what you broke” (see pitfall below). Frame it as: “You lit fires for people who would have frozen. You knew the rule and you chose mercy. That choice deserves a successor act — not punishment, not forgiveness. Combat Action.” Kaelen wants her transgression to have meant something. She lit those fires. If she can act now in a way that flows from that decision, the twelve years of guilt become a preparation rather than a sentence.

  • Duty (the mark still glows): The Ever-Ember chose her at thirteen. It has not chosen another in twelve years. The ember-mark on her forehead still burns — she is still a Keeper whether she accepts the title or not. The office doesn’t expire when the Keeper flees. Kaelen bristles at this but cannot dismiss it: the evidence is written into her own face.

  • Protecting the common folk (make it specific): “The people of Hrafnborg are going to die. Not soldiers. Not kings. Families. Children.” The same instinct that made her light fires for a sick village is the instinct the party must reach. Make the human cost real and specific — name people they’ve met, describe what they’ve seen on the road. Abstract stakes don’t move her. Named people do.

Pitfalls (automatic failure — Interest -1, Patience -1):

  • Glory: “You’ll be a hero.” Kaelen does not want to be a hero. She wants to have done something worthy, which is entirely different. Framing her return as fame or legend reduces a moral question to a performance opportunity. She closes off immediately.

  • Orders from authority: “The King commands it.” Kaelen left a structure of institutional authority that gave her rules and no one to ask when she needed guidance. Any argument that boils down to “because someone above you says so” triggers defiance rather than compliance—even though she respects Harald personally.

  • Minimizing her guilt (the worst pitfall): “It wasn’t your fault” or “You’re being too hard on yourself.” Her guilt is real, founded on something true, and she has examined it for twelve years. Anyone who dismisses it is dismissing the evidence of her own experience. This is the most dangerous pitfall—it reads as condescension and tells her the PCs don’t understand what they’re asking. Avoid it entirely.

Anyone who dismisses it is dismissing the evidence of her own experience. This is the most dangerous pitfall—it reads as condescension and tells her the PCs don’t understand what they’re asking. Negotiation Flow

The PCs can discuss strategy between arguments (the “out of character planning” approach is explicitly supported by Draw Steel). Let them feel the weight of Patience 2—they have very few swings.

Presence: Emotional appeals, empathy, personal connection. Best for Redemption and Common Folk arguments.

Reason: Logical arguments, strategic framing, evidence. Works well for Duty and specific-stakes arguments.

Intuition: Reading Kaelen’s responses mid-negotiation. Can be used to pivot when a line of argument isn’t landing.

Lew’s contribution: If the PCs allow it, Lew can make one argument using Presence +3. He uses it like this: “I don’t know what you did. I don’t care. I know what it’s like to lose something you can’t get back and spend years deciding if you deserved to. The question isn’t whether you deserve a second chance. The question is whether Hrafnborg deserves to die because you’re afraid of one.”

Lew’s argument always hits moderate roll minimum (Presence +3 on a medium roll). It raises Interest by 1—but it also costs 1 Patience. Kaelen resents being managed, even expertly. A wise party uses Lew only if they’re in trouble and have Patience to spare.

Reading Kaelen’s Responses

Use these to guide roleplay between arguments:

  • Interest goes up: Kaelen goes quiet. Her jaw unclenches. She looks at the ground rather than at the PCs—this is her processing, not disengagement. “I hear you” is the highest praise she’ll offer in the moment.

  • Interest stays flat (no-motivation argument): She sighs. “You’re repeating yourself.” Or: “That’s not an argument. That’s a hope.”

  • Interest drops (pitfall): Her eyes go flat. Her voice drops to something controlled and cold. “Say that again and I’ll ask the dryads to show you out.”

  • Patience at 1: She checks the sun. Looks at the thorn-wall. Her body has already started making the calculation between “this conversation” and “the grove that needs me.” The PCs should feel the pressure.

Bonus from Beat 6a/6b

If the PCs protected 2+ living roots during the Rootgnaw Brood combat (Beat 6), the Green Heart’s root-blessing grants +2 to Presence tests during this negotiation. The grove’s approval is visible—small flowers bloom near the PCs' feet, and the dryads relax their stance.

Part 5: Outcomes

Negotiation Success (Interest reaches 5)

Kaelen agrees to come—but on her terms.

Kaelen is quiet for a long time. The amber light of the grove pulses around her. The ember-mark on her forehead brightens—not with warmth, but with something like recognition.

"Fine," she says. Not surrendering. Deciding. "I’ll come. I’ll speak to the Ever-Ember, if it’ll still speak to me. But I’m not going back to be a Keeper. I’m going back to fix what I broke. And when it’s fixed—if it can be fixed—I’m coming back here."

She stands. Looks at you. For the first time, the grief in her eyes has company: something harder, sharper.

Resolve.

"Don’t make me regret this."

Conditions of success:

  • Kaelen joins the party as an NPC ally for the journey back.

  • She insists on saying goodbye to the dryads (1 hour).

  • She brings nothing from the grove—"I’m not taking from this place. I’ve taken enough."

  • She cooperates fully but remains guarded. Earning her real trust will take Acts 2 and 3.

Negotiation Failure (Patience reduced to 0)

Kaelen refuses. She’s made her choice.

"I said no." Kaelen’s voice is flat. "I’ve heard your arguments. All of them. And I understand. But I can’t—"

She stops. Looks past you, toward the thorn-wall.

The dryads are screaming.

The negotiation is interrupted by an attack. Whether the PCs failed or simply ran out of time, something breaches the Green Heart’s boundary: Pale Maw agents (directed by Grafvitnir’s network acting on Dreyfus’s intelligence from Hrafnborg), Rootgnaw creatures drawn by the disturbance, or a Rot-warped landvaettr—a corrupted nature spirit that the dryads can’t stop alone.

The Attack That Convinces Her

Enemies (Option A — Pale Maw Assault, EV 118):

  • 3 Pale Maw Acolytes (Level 2 Ambusher Elite)

  • 2 Pale Maw Zealots (Level 3 Harrier Elite)

  • 3 Rot-Warped Landvaettrs (Level 3 Controller Platoon — corrupted tree-spirits, once dryad allies)

Enemies (Option B — Rootgnaw Brood, EV 114):

  • 12 Rootgnaw Grubs (Level 1 Harrier Minion)

  • 3 Rootgnaw Queens (Level 3 Controller Platoon)

  • 3 Vine Stranglers (Level 2 Ambusher Elite — corrupted plants from the Ashen Woods fringe)

The PCs must fight to protect the grove. Kaelen fights alongside them—not as an ally (yet) but as a defender of her home. Use her NPC stat block from the MODULE.adoc.

The Aftermath of the Attack

milestones — Type: Combat (Conditional - only if negotiation fails and Kaelen is threatened) | Difficulty: Moderate to Hard | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

milestones — Type: Combat (Conditional - only if negotiation fails and Kaelen is threatened) | Difficulty: Moderate to Hard | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

When it’s over, Kaelen stands in the ruined clearing, breathing hard. A dryad lies broken among the roots—not dead, but hurt. Sap runs from its bark like blood.

Kaelen kneels beside the dryad and presses her hand to the wound. The ember-mark flares. The dryad’s bark seals.

She looks up at you, and her expression has changed. The fear is still there. But so is anger.

"They found this place," she says. "They found the only living thing left in these woods, and they attacked it."

She stands.

"Alright. I’ll come. Not for Hrafnborg. Not for the King. For this." She gestures at the wounded grove. "Because if I stay, they’ll keep coming. And the Green Heart can’t survive that."

Conditions of failure-to-success:

  • Kaelen joins, but her motivation is anger, not duty. She is less cooperative and more volatile.

  • She does not trust the party as quickly—they failed to convince her with words, and that stings.

  • The attack itself is evidence that Grafvitnir’s network knows about the Green Heart. Dreyfus overheard the party’s plans in Hrafnborg and relayed the information via Wyrd-channels. This accelerates the Grafvitnir timeline.

  • Mechanically: Kaelen starts with 1 fewer hit die (emotional/physical cost of the fight) and her Interest in future negotiations with the party starts at 1 instead of 2.

Part 6: The Farewell

Whether Kaelen agreed through words or was forced by violence, her departure from the Green Heart is a moment.

Kaelen walks the perimeter of the grove one last time. She touches each tree. She speaks to each dryad in a language you don’t understand—older than Norrœnt, older than anything you’ve heard. The words sound like roots growing.

The dryads don’t cry. But the leaves around them darken, and the birdsong fades to silence.

At the thorn-wall, Kaelen stops. She presses her palm to the brambles. They part for her—slowly, reluctantly—and on the other side is the grey.

"Don’t look back," the lead dryad says. Not to Kaelen. To you. "If she looks back, she won’t leave."

Kaelen doesn’t look back.

You step through. The thorns close behind you. And the warmth of the Green Heart cuts off like a door slamming shut.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Entered the Green Heart and experienced genuine beauty in a dead world.

  • Met Kaelen and understood the weight of her guilt and her choice.

  • Successfully negotiated her return (or been forced to fight alongside her when the Heart was attacked).

  • Understood that Kaelen’s guilt is connected to the Ever-Ember crisis (though the full truth is still hidden).

  • Gained a powerful NPC ally—reluctant, complicated, and carrying secrets.

  • Felt the grove’s farewell as a genuine loss.

Overview

The return journey. The PCs travel west from the Green Heart through deteriorating terrain toward Hrafnborg, now with Kaelen and Lew in tow.

Distance: ~220 miles
Duration: ~12 days — blended terrain rates (24 miles/day on roads, 18 mildly hindered, 12 severely hindered in mountain passes)
Calendar: Departure approximately Heyannir 3rd; arrival approximately Heyannir 14th–15th. Early winter is setting in — the first true cold of Vetr bleeds into the tail of Sumar. The days shorten noticeably during this leg.

The road is familiar but changed. The weather has worsened. The undead are more active and organized—moving in columns toward Hrafnborg. Grafvitnir’s network is closing.

This beat serves three purposes:

  1. Character development: Time with Kaelen and Lew as real people, not quest objectives.

  2. Growing threat: The world is visibly deteriorating. The Ever-Embers are failing faster.

  3. Gragnir’s reveal: The druid appears and exposes the king’s hound — Dreyfus is a shape-walker spy serving Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf — and the horror is that he’s still in Hrafnborg, over two hundred miles away, at Harald’s side.

Part 1: Leaving the Green Heart

The thorn-wall closes behind you. One moment, green — light, birdsong, the smell of living earth. The next: ash. Silence. Grey.

Kaelen walks at the rear of the group. She hasn’t spoken since the wall sealed. Lew, for once, doesn’t fill the silence. His eye is dim—the amber barely a glow. "She doesn’t want to be tracked," he murmurs to you. "The Heart is hiding itself. Even from me."

The dead trees stand like gravestones. And you walk.

Twelve days. Over two hundred miles. The longest road yet.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Return Route

The party retraces the general path of their outbound journey, but the landscape has changed in the days since they passed. The world is deteriorating faster now — Heyannir marks the transition from late summer into early winter under Fimbulwinter conditions. What was merely cold on the outbound trek is now bitter. What was passable is now dangerous.

If the party took the Ashen Woods outbound, they exit the Green Heart into familiar (but worsening) charcoal forest, then cross the Dead Farms and the mountains.

If the party took the Bone Fields outbound, they may choose to return that way (shorter but exposed) or swing through the Ashen Woods (longer but with more shelter). Lew recommends the Ashen Woods for the return: "I don’t want to cross the Fields twice. And the Woods have cover. If what Gragnir said about the dead moving southwest is true, cover matters."

Either way, the montage structure below works. Adjust geographic descriptions to match the outbound route.

Between Rounds 1 and 2, seed these observations. The world has deteriorated measurably in the ~10 days since the party passed through:

The clearing where you camped on the outbound journey is different. The charcoal trunks you used as windbreaks have fallen. The fire-pit you dug is full of grey ice.

Part 4: Point of Interest — The Dead Waystation

Place this between Round 1 and Round 2, as the party exits the Ashen Woods / wasteland and enters the Interior Belt.

You come to a crossroads you remember from the outbound journey — or think you do. The signpost is gone. The road is buried under a foot of frozen mud. But the waystation at the crossroads is still standing: a stone shelter with a turf roof, built to house travelers and stock feed for horses.

Inside: bodies.

Not undead. Not risen. Just dead. Seven people — a family, by their clothing. Two adults, three children, two elders. They died huddled together around a fire that went out. Their supplies are scattered but mostly intact: grain, salted fish, a good wool blanket, a medicine box.

They didn’t die of cold. They died of despair. The elder’s hand still holds a knife. There are marks on the floor — tally marks, scratched into stone. Fourteen days' worth.

They waited for help that never came.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: The Dead Waystation

This is pure narrative weight. No attribute test, no loot grab, no moral choice (though the PCs may want to bury them — let them).

The point: fourteen days ago, when the PCs were heading south toward the Green Heart, this family was alive and waiting. If the PCs had stopped, if they’d come this way, if they’d done something… but they didn’t. They couldn’t have. They didn’t know.

This is the cost of Fimbulwinter. Not monsters. Not evil. Just cold and time and silence.

If the PCs bury the family (takes 2 hours, costs nothing, earns no mechanical benefit): Kaelen helps dig. She doesn’t speak, but she works harder than anyone. Lew finds a prayer-stone in the medicine box and places it on the cairn.

The party gains no Milestones from this. But it matters.

Part 5: Character Development Scenes

Distribute these scenes across the journey — between montage rounds, during rest stops, on the march. They should feel organic, not staged.

Scene: Kaelen and the Ember-Mark

Around the campfire (such as it is), Kaelen touches the scar on her forehead. It glows faintly—more than it did in the Green Heart, as if distance from the grove makes it brighter.

"It never went away," she says, to no one in particular. "I thought it would. I hoped it would. But the Ever-Ember marks you forever. Even if you run."

She looks at the fire—your small, mortal campfire.

"Do you know what it feels like? When the Ember chooses you? It’s not warm. It’s heavy. Like someone put the weight of every person who’ll ever stand near that fire on your shoulders, and said: 'Don’t drop this.'"

She pulls her hand away from the scar.

"I dropped it."

Let the PCs respond. Kaelen doesn’t want comfort—she wants to be heard. If a PC shares their own failure or regret, Kaelen listens carefully and nods. She doesn’t open up further, but a connection forms.

Scene: Lew and Gragnir

If the PCs ask Lew about Gragnir (or he volunteers during a quiet march):

"Gragnir’s not what you think he is," Lew says, adjusting his pack. "He’s not a wizard. He’s not a hermit. He’s a—" He searches for the word. "He’s a gardener. The world is his garden. And right now, the garden is on fire and he can’t put it out alone."

A pause.

"He talks to trees. That sounds mad, I know. But I’ve seen it. We were in the Bone-Fields—years ago, bad job, long story—and the ground split open and something came out of the earth. Something old. And Gragnir walked up to it and talked to it. Not magic. Not a mystical power. Just… spoke. Like he was speaking to something that understood."

He taps his amber eye. "He gave me this after. Said, 'You’ve paid enough for one lifetime, Lew. This is change, not compensation.' I didn’t know what he meant then."

A grim smile. "I still don’t, most days."

Scene: Kaelen and Lew

Let this happen naturally—around a fire, during a march, wherever it fits:

Kaelen eyes Lew’s amber eye. Lew notices her noticing.

"It’s a gem," he says. "Not glass."

"I know what it is," Kaelen says quietly. "I can feel it. It resonates with—" She stops. Touches her ember-mark. "With the same frequency. The same hum."

Lew raises an eyebrow. "Gragnir gave it to me."

"Of course he did." Kaelen almost smiles. Almost. "That man never gives gifts. He gives obligations."

This scene establishes that Kaelen’s ember-mark and Lew’s eye-gem share a connection—both are linked to Yggdrasil’s root network. This will become important in Act 2.

Part 6: Montage Test — Round 2: The Interior Belt (Days 5–8)

The Dead Farms. The PCs remember them from the outbound journey — grey fields, empty crofts, silence. But the Interior Belt has gotten worse.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate through refugee-clogged roads Reason / Presence The roads the PCs used outbound are now packed with refugees heading north — away from Hrafnborg. Word has spread that the dead are gathering. The PCs are heading into the flow.
Forage in a depleted landscape Intuition The Dead Farms have been picked clean by refugees and worse. Finding food, fuel, or shelter requires creativity—old root cellars, frozen caches, hunting what little game remains.
Cross a collapsing bridge Agility / Might The river bridge they crossed outbound has partially collapsed. Supports rotted by the grey water. Options: repair (medium Reason test), climb across debris (easy Agility test), ford the river (medium Might test but costs Heat from cold exposure).
Avoid undead patrol columns Agility / Intuition The dead are moving in organized columns now — dozens of draugr, walking southwest, heading toward Hrafnborg. They don’t hunt the PCs specifically, but their routes intersect. A wrong turn puts the party in a column’s path.
Maintain supply discipline Reason Twelve days is long. Eight days in, supplies are thinning. Rationing now affects morale (Presence tests at second chance (two rolls, take worst) if rations are cut) but preserves options.
Keep the party together emotionally Presence Kaelen is volatile. Lew is worried about Kaelen. The PCs are tired. Managing morale and preventing friction is its own challenge.

Round 2 Interlude: The World is Worse — Part II

Seed these through the Interior Belt crossing:

  • A village they passed outbound is empty. Not abandoned—empty. Doors open. Food on tables. No people, no bodies. Just absence. Footprints in the frost lead southwest — toward the draugr columns. The implication is horrifying: the living followed the dead. Or became them.

  • The sky is darker. Not night—a grey-brown overcast that doesn’t lift. The sun is a pale disc, barely visible. Kaelen says the Ever-Ember at Hrafnborg is "straining — I can feel it from here."

  • Rivers that were frozen outbound are now unfrozen — not because of warmth, but because the ice is rotting. The water beneath is grey and doesn’t freeze properly anymore. It smells of iron and decay.

  • At one point, the party passes a line of draugr walking southwest. Dozens of them — not attacking, not hunting, just walking. All heading toward Hrafnborg. They ignore the party completely, as if following instructions from elsewhere.

The column stretches back further than you can see. They walk in rough formation — three abreast, shields hanging loose, weapons rusted but functional. Their eyes are the dead grey of bog ice.

Kaelen watches them pass. "They’re not just moving," she says. "They’re mustering. Someone is calling them."

Lew touches his eye. "Southwest. Always southwest. Same direction as Hrafnborg."

The column takes twenty minutes to pass. None of the dead look at you. They have orders. You are not part of them.

Kaelen’s ember-mark flares once, bright enough to cast a shadow. Two or three draugr turn their heads — slowly, mechanically — and Kaelen steps back and presses her palm over the mark, hiding the light.

The draugr turn away. The column moves on.

"Don’t do that again," Lew says quietly.

Part 7: Negotiation — The Holdfast at Greyfen

milestones — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success

Place this between Round 2 and Round 3. As the party approaches the mountain pass, they scene a fortified refugee camp.

Context

The road to Midgard’s Shield passes through Greyfen — or what’s left of it. The village itself is a ruin: burned timbers, caved roofs, a broken well. But someone has fortified the ruins. Barricades of stone and timber ring the village centre. Sharpened stakes face outward. Torches burn along an improvised parapet.

A voice calls from behind the barricade.

"That’s far enough. State your business or take the long way round."

You count maybe thirty people behind the walls. Civilians — families, farmers, merchants — but armed. Badly, desperately armed. Kitchen knives lashed to staves. Hunting bows with mismatched arrows. A few proper blades, notched and dull.

They’re terrified. And terrified people are dangerous.

The Negotiation

The Holdfast is led by Thyra Blackhand — a former Chainwarden sergeant who deserted her post when the dead began moving and gathered survivors to this fortified position.

Negotiation: Moderate
Interest: 3 (Wary but willing to listen — she needs allies, even if she won’t admit it)
Patience: 2 (how long before Thyra tells them to leave or the mob turns hostile)

What the PCs Want

  • Passage through Greyfen — the barricades block the most direct road to the Titan’s Stair pass. Going around adds 2 days.

  • Information — Thyra knows the mountain pass conditions. She may also have news about Hrafnborg.

  • Supplies — the Holdfast is better-provisioned than the PCs expect (they raided a granary).

What Thyra Wants

  • News from the south — she’s heard nothing from beyond the Interior Belt in weeks.

  • Reassurance that Hrafnborg stands — if the city has fallen, her plan to evacuate north is pointless.

  • A solution for the dead column — a draugr column is expected to pass Greyfen within 24 hours. She doesn’t know if the barricades will hold.

Motivations and Pitfalls

Motivation Description
Hope (Interest +1) If the PCs share that the Green Heart still lives, or that Kaelen bears the Ember-mark, Thyra’s people rally. Hope is rare. This earns immediate trust.
Pragmatism (Interest +1) Offering practical help — tactics for the draugr column, use of fire as a weapon, lending physical labour to reinforce the barricades — speaks Thyra’s language.
Authority (Interest +0, Patience −1) Invoking the King’s name or official authority gets a cold response. Thyra deserted the Chainwardens. She doesn’t trust institutions anymore.
Threats (Patience −2) Any attempt to force passage triggers immediate hostility. Thyra has nothing to lose. Her people are ready to die defending what little they have.
Kaelen’s Presence (Second Chance (two rolls, take best) on Arguments) If Kaelen is present and reveals her ember-mark, the effect is immediate and powerful. Several refugees weep. One elderly man kneels. Thyra stares for a long time. "An Ember-Keeper. Walking south. In Fimbulwinter." She exhales. "Maybe we’re not done yet." For the remainder of this negotiation, all party members gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on argument rolls.

Outcomes

  • Interest filled: Thyra opens the gate. She provides passage, trail rations (3 days' worth), and critical intelligence: the Titan’s Stair is passable but treacherous — ice has claimed the upper switchbacks and a rockslide has narrowed the pass. She also shares a warning: "The dead aren’t random. They’re converging. Everything’s flowing toward Hrafnborg like water downhill." Before the PCs leave, Thyra pulls them aside: "If Hrafnborg falls, we fall. You know that. Get to the city. get to the Ember. And hold it."

  • Patience exhausted: Thyra orders the PCs away. Going around adds 2 days and denies them the supply bonus and intelligence. If the PCs push, combat begins—but fighting refugees earns no Milestones and costs 1 Victory if they harm anyone.

Thyra Blackhand is a potential recurring ally. If the PCs help her in any material way (tactics, supplies, even just honest news), she survives the draugr column and leads her people to Hrafnborg, arriving during or after the siege. In Act 2, she becomes a valuable military contact — a sergeant who knows how to fight the dead.

If the PCs ignore Greyfen or fail the negotiation badly, Thyra’s holdfast falls to the draugr column. The PCs may find the aftermath on a later journey: barricades broken, bodies frozen, stakes still standing.

Part 8: Final Approach — Mountain Crossing to Hrafnborg

The final phase. The PCs retrace their path through Midgard’s Shield — the mountain range separating the Interior Belt from the coast. The Titan’s Stair pass is their route back.

The weather has turned. This is the first true blizzard of early winter. Heyannir is the threshold month—the last of Sumar giving way to Vetr. The Fimbulwinter makes the transition savage.

Narrative Interlude: The View from the Summit

At the summit of Titan’s Stair, the wind drops. Just for a moment—a gap between gusts, a breath of stillness in the blizzard.

And from the summit, looking south toward the coast, you see it.

Hrafnborg.

The Ever-Ember’s light is visible even through the storm—a warm amber glow on the horizon, impossibly bright and impossibly small. Like a candle in a cathedral.

But around it: darkness. Not storm-darkness. Something else. A stain on the land, surrounding the city in every direction. From this distance, it could be shadow. It could be an army.

Kaelen’s ember-mark blazes. She gasps and grabs at her forehead.

"It’s still lit," she says. "The Ember’s still burning." She pauses. "But it’s screaming. Something is pressing against it. Something cold and old and patient."

Lew squints into the distance. "What am I looking at? Is that—are those—"

"Yes," Kaelen says. "The dead."

The wind returns. The summit vanishes in white. The PCs begin the descent.

Part 9: Montage Outcomes (Round 1–2)

Total Success (5+ successes before 3 failures)

  • Return journey is swift and efficient. The party arrives near Hrafnborg with supplies, energy, and time.

  • Earn 2 Milestones.

  • The undead column is behind them—they’ve outpaced the dead.

  • Kaelen is shaken but steady. She’s ready to face the Ember.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • The party reaches the outskirts of Hrafnborg, but at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Rations are gone. The party is starving by the time they sight Hrafnborg.

    • Time cost: The return took longer than expected. The undead column is ahead of them, and Hrafnborg is already under pressure when they arrive.

    • Injury cost: One PC was injured in the mountain crossing—a fall, a rockslide, a frostbiten limb. They arrive with 1 fewer Hit Die.

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party arrives but is exhausted and compromised.

    • All PCs and NPCs lose 1 Hit Die.

    • The undead army has already reached Hrafnborg. The siege has begun and the PCs are outside the walls.

    • No Milestones earned. Director gains 4 Malice for the Act 1 climax.

Part 10: Gragnir’s Appearance — The Reveal

This is the turning point of Beat 8 and the setup for Act 1’s climax.

When It Happens

After the montage test concludes, as the party camps within a day’s travel of Hrafnborg — near the base of the mountains on the coastal side. The fire burns low. The night is cold. The PCs can see the distant glow of Hrafnborg’s Ever-Ember on the horizon—or perhaps the glow of fires that aren’t Ever-Embers at all.

The Arrival

The fire pops. A log shifts.

And Gragnir is there.

He’s sitting on the other side of the fire, as if he’s been there for hours. His soot-stained hands rest on his knees. His grey furs are dusted with ash. He looks tired—not the fatigue of travel, but the exhaustion of someone who has been fighting a war no one else can see.

"You found her," he says, looking at Kaelen. His voice is rough. "Good."

Kaelen stiffens. "Gragnir."

"Kaelen."

They look at each other across the fire. Something old and uncomfortable passes between them.

"We need to talk," Gragnir says. "All of us. Now. Before tomorrow."

Gragnir’s Warning

Gragnir speaks plainly for the first time. He is done with riddles.

"Your King has a companion," Gragnir says quietly. He stares into the fire. "A grey hound. Sits at his feet during every council. Sleeps at the foot of his bed. Follows him to the Ever-Ember chamber. Never barks. Never eats. Just watches."

His black fingers tighten on his knees.

"It is not a dog. It is not a wolf. It is a shape-walker — a fragment given form. A spy. And it has been listening to every word spoken in the King’s presence since before you arrived in Hrafnborg."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"It is listening right now. Over two hundred miles away. Sitting beside your King in the dark."

Let the PCs react. The crucial horror: Dreyfus is not here. He is not at the edge of the firelight. He is not on the road behind them. He is in Hrafnborg, at Harald’s side, and has been the entire time. Every conversation the King has had since they left — about the mission, about troop movements, about the city’s defences — Dreyfus has heard. And through the Wyrd-channels — root-whispers carried through Yggdrasil’s dying network — that intelligence flows directly to Grafvitnir’s agents in the field.

The PCs cannot confront Dreyfus. They cannot warn Harald. They are over two hundred miles from Hrafnborg, and the spy is still whispering.

The Truth About Grafvitnir

"Its master’s name is Grafvitnir," Gragnir says. The word falls like a stone. "The Grave-Wolf. An entity older than Svellheim, older than the Ember-Keepers, older than anything that walks above ground."

He stands. The fire dips as if his shadow weighs more than it should.

"Grafvitnir was imprisoned during the First Kindling—when the original Ember-Keepers sealed the root-chamber beneath Yggdrasil’s deepest root. It was not killed. It cannot be killed. It was bound—by fire, by oath, by the roots of the World Tree itself."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"Every Ever-Ember that fails is a lock that opens. Every Keeper who dies is a chain that breaks. When the last Ember goes dark—Grafvitnir walks free. And when the Grave-Wolf walks, the dead do not rest. Not in their graves. Not anywhere."

If someone asks Gragnir how the binding works — how fire can hold something like the Grave-Wolf in the earth — he picks up a handful of ash from the fire’s second chance (two rolls, take best) and squeezes it in his fist.

“Think of it as... the space between the world and the dark place underneath. Not a wall. Not a door. A substance. Thick, like the fat on a cold stew. While the Embers burn, the substance is dense — nothing can push through it. Nothing below can reach up. Nothing dead can crawl out.”

He opens his hand. The ash sifts through his fingers.

“But every Ember that goes out — the substance thins. Things begin to seep. Small things first. Cold. Whispers. The dead stirring in their barrows. Then bigger things. Bolder things. Things with teeth and purpose.”

He dusts his palm on his knee.

“When the last Ember fails, the substance is gone. There is nothing between the Grave-Wolf and the sky.”

ðŸ“ Director Note: The Barrier Metaphor

This is the campaign’s core metaphysical explanation. The binding is not a cage or a chain — it is a barrier substance, a dense membrane between realms. Strong flame = thick barrier. Weak flame = thin barrier. No flame = no barrier. Every supernatural symptom the party has encountered — the dead farms, the Ashen Woods decay, the Pale Maw’s growing boldness, the cold that seeps up from below — is a consequence of the barrier thinning. When Kaelen eventually transforms at the Green Heart, she is not merely “sealing locks” — she is restoring the barrier’s density, becoming the substance itself. This is the framework Gragnir gives them. It should inform how they understand everything that follows.

The Horror of Distance

There is no confrontation. There is no shape-shifting escape. There is only the dawning understanding that the enemy’s greatest weapon is over two hundred miles behind them, curled at the feet of their King, and they cannot do a single thing about it.

Kaelen’s face goes white. "He heard everything. When Harald told us about the Ever-Ember. When he gave us our mission. Every name, every route, every supply count."

Lew is very still. "That’s how they always knew where we were. Not a tracker on the road. A listener in the throne room."

Gragnir nods.

"It has been reporting since before you left Hrafnborg. Everything you told the King, Grafvitnir knows. Your mission. Your route. Your allies." He looks at Kaelen. "Especially you."

"And when you return," he says, "the hound will still be there. Sitting. Watching. It cannot act directly — guest-right binds even shape-walkers. But it does not need to act. It only needs to listen."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Gragnir After the Reveal

Gragnir doesn’t stay long. He came to deliver this warning and to prepare the PCs for what’s ahead.

The key dramatic tension: the party now KNOWS about Dreyfus, but Dreyfus doesn’t know they know. When they return to Hrafnborg, they must decide how to handle this — warn Harald openly (which alerts Dreyfus), try to act in secret, or simply endure the spy’s presence while they work. This tension carries into Act 2.

If the PCs ask questions:

  • "Why didn’t you tell us sooner?" — "Because you needed to see the world first. The dead farms. The Ashen Woods. The Green Heart. You needed to understand what’s dying before I could explain why it’s dying."

  • "Can we stop Grafvitnir?" — "Not yet. Not with what you are now. But you can slow him. The Ever-Ember at Hrafnborg still burns. If Kaelen can speak to it—truly speak—it may tell us how the bindings can be renewed."

  • "What does Grafvitnir want?" — "Freedom. And then? The end of everything green, everything warm, everything that remembers the light. The Grave-Wolf does not hate life. It simply does not recognize it as valid."

  • "Can we expose the hound?" — "You can try. But guest-right is ancient law. Even Harald — especially Harald — cannot simply destroy a creature under his roof. And the shape-walker is clever. If it feels cornered, it will act. Better to know where the spy sits than to drive it into hiding."

  • "Where have you been?" — "Tending what’s left. The roots are sick, not dead. I have been speaking to them—asking them to hold on a little longer. They’re listening. Barely."

  • "What’s our next move?" — "Get to Hrafnborg. Get Kaelen to the Ever-Ember. And pray the flame still recognizes her voice. As for the hound… you’ll have to decide what to do about that yourselves."

Gragnir’s Price

As Gragnir rises to leave, he pauses. He looks at the fire — not at the party, not at Kaelen. At the fire.

“One more thing.” His voice is quieter now. Not softer — quieter, the way a blade is quiet before it cuts.

“Your King gave you a mission. Find the Keeper. Bring her back. Good. Simple. A king’s errand.”

He turns to face the party.

“But the roots don’t answer to kings. Yggdrasil doesn’t care about Hrafnborg’s walls or Harald’s council or who sits on what throne. The tree cares about surviving. The binding cares about holding. And when those two things conflict with your King’s orders — when serving the roots means defying the crown — you will have to choose.”

He holds out his hand, palm up. Soot falls from his fingers like black snow.

“Swear to me. Not to Harald. Not to the Konungs-fer—. Swear to the roots themselves: that when the moment comes, you will serve Yggdrasil’s renewal above all other obligations. Above politics. Above loyalty. Above every comfortable promise you’ve made to comfortable people in comfortable rooms.”

He waits. The fire pops.

“If you cannot swear this, I will still help you. But I will know what you are. And so will the roots.”

ðŸ“ Director Note: The Root-Oath and Its Cost

This oath directly conflicts with the Konungs-fer— oath sworn to Harald in Beat 3. The party swore to serve the King’s mission. Now Gragnir asks them to swear that, if forced to choose, they will serve the roots over the crown.

This is not hypothetical. The conflict will manifest:

  • In Act 2, when the party must decide whether to pursue the mission as Harald defined it (bring Kaelen back to Hrafnborg) or follow the roots’ guidance (which leads Kaelen north, away from the city, toward the Emberwell)
  • In Act 3, when Hrafnborg’s political survival and Yggdrasil’s ecological survival may require different sacrifices

If the party swears the Root-Oath, they gain Gragnir’s full trust and future cooperation. He will appear when the roots tell him to, and he will share information freely.

If the party refuses, Gragnir nods once. “Honest, at least. I can work with honest.” He will still help, but he will withhold certain information — specifically, he will not reveal the location of the Green Heart until the party proves their commitment through actions rather than words.

Word economy: This is a binding oath. If the party swears and later chooses Harald’s orders over the roots’ needs, they lose 2 Word permanently and Gragnir will not answer their calls.

Gragnir departs before dawn. He does not say goodbye. He says: "I’ll find you when the roots tell me to."

Kaelen watches him go with an expression that is equal parts respect and resentment.

"That man has never once explained himself before it was almost too late," she says. "Not once."

Part 11: Level Up

After the reveal, with Hrafnborg visible on the horizon and the full weight of the crisis now understood:

The party advances to Level 3.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Level-Up Moment

Frame this as: the PCs have crossed a threshold. They started as local volunteers answering a summons. Now they understand the shape of the crisis—the dying tree, the bound evil, the failing embers. They aren’t travelers anymore. They’re agents of the flame.

Level 3 in Draw Steel means access to more powerful abilities and better survivability. They’ll need both for what’s coming.

Part 12: Approach to Hrafnborg

The final scene of Beat 8: the PCs crest the last ridge before Hrafnborg and see what awaits them.

You see Hrafnborg at dawn. The Ever-Ember’s glow is visible from miles away—a small orange star in a grey world.

But around it: darkness.

The walls are intact. The gates are closed. Fires burn along the ramparts—not warmth, but signal-fires. And beyond the walls, in a ring that stretches from the coast to the foothills, the dead stand.

Thousands of them. Draugr, ghosts, barrow-wights, frost-walkers—a silent army of the restless dead, shoulder to shoulder, unmoving. Not attacking. Not retreating.

Waiting.

Hrafnborg is under siege.

  • Crossed over two hundred miles of deteriorating terrain and bonded with Kaelen and Lew.

  • Witnessed the world’s accelerating collapse: empty villages, draugr columns, failing infrastructure.

  • Negotiated with survivors at Greyfen and seen how desperate the common folk have become.

  • Learned the truth about Dreyfus (shape-walker spy still at Harald’s side, Níðhöggr’s eyes and ears) and the deeper threat (Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf, imprisoned and weakening).

  • Seen Hrafnborg under siege—the Act 1 climax is here.

Overview

The Act 1 climax. Hrafnborg is encircled by thousands of silent undead — not attacking, not retreating, simply waiting. The Ever-Ember burns lower every hour. The city is cut off, supplies are dwindling, and morale is crumbling.

The PCs must:

  2. Reunite with Harald and witness the besieged city’s desperation.

  3. Bring Kaelen to the Ever-Ember and witness her attempt to speak to the flame.

  4. Defend the ritual when the undead sense what Kaelen is doing and breach the walls (Boss combat).

This beat ends Act 1 and pivots the campaign from find the Keeper to save the World-Tree.

Part 1: The Silent Ring

The PCs stand on the last ridge before Hrafnborg. Beat 8 ended with the image of the siege. Now they must reckon with it up close.

From the ridge, the scale of it hits you.

The dead surround Hrafnborg in a ring three bodies deep. Draugr stand shoulder to shoulder, frost-blue and motionless, their burial shrouds barely stirring. Behind them, taller shapes — barrow-wights in rusted hauberks, spectral figures that flicker at the edge of sight. And farther back, in the fog off the fjord, darker things move — vast and slow.

None of them make a sound.

The city’s walls are intact. Signal-fires burn along the ramparts. You can see guards — living guards — pacing the wall-walk, their torch-light catching on spear-tips. The Ever-Ember’s glow is visible above the roofline: a faint orange star, dimmer than it should be.

No one is getting in. No one is getting out.

Reconnaissance

Before attempting the breach, the PCs can observe the siege line. Allow Cunning and tests:

  • Easy Intuition test: The undead are densest at the main gates and the harbour approach. The southwestern stretch — near the old tanneries — is thinner. The ground there is marshy and unstable, which the dead seem to avoid.

  • Medium Reason test: The siege isn’t tactical in the normal sense. There are no siege engines, no trenches, no supply lines. This is a spiritual blockade — the dead are drawn to the Ever-Ember like moths to a candle, pressing against its warmth. They aren’t commanded. They’re compelled.

  • Easy Might test: A group this size means the barrow-fields for fifty miles around have emptied. This isn’t a local uprising — this is every grave in the Coastal Belt.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: The Cunning of the Siege

The dead are not under Grafvitnir’s direct command here. The Grave-Wolf is still imprisoned — but as the Ever-Embers weaken, the boundary between life and death thins. The dead simply rise and are drawn to the largest remaining source of warmth.

This is important thematically: the siege isn’t an army with a general. It’s a symptom. A fever. The dead are the immune response of a dying world.

Some GM options for flavour:

  • The dead occasionally shift, like a crowd swaying. No individual moves — the whole ring breathes.

  • If anyone shouts, the nearest draugr turn their heads. Slowly. Then turn back.

  • A few of the dead are recent. Fresh faces. Villagers who died in the last week.

Kaelen’s Reaction

Kaelen stares at the siege line. Her ember-mark flickers — the scar on her forehead glowing, then dimming, in rhythm with the distant Ever-Ember.

"It’s calling them," Kaelen whispers. Her hand goes to her forehead, pressing the ember-mark. "The flame is so weak it’s pulling instead of pushing. It’s trying to feed, and all it can find is… the dead."

She looks at you. Her eyes are wet, and her voice is steel.

"We have to get in there. Now."

Lew’s Assessment

Lew studies the line with his amber eye, which pulses faintly.

"The southwest, near the tannery ditches. The ground’s soft — even dead legs don’t like mud. The line’s two deep there instead of three, and the wights are clustered near the gates, not the margins."

He pauses. "It’s not a door. But it’s the closest thing to one."

Part 2: Breaking the Line (Hard Montage Test)

milestones — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 2 VP on hard success, 1 VP success, 0 VP failure (2 rounds)

The party must cross the siege ring and reach Hrafnborg’s walls. The southwestern approach through the tannery marshes is the best route — but "best" is relative.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes + 2 NPC companions):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds. A combat complication triggers between rounds.

NPC Contributions:

  • Kaelen: Assists one PC’s test per round (Might +3 or Presence +2). She can suppress her ember-mark’s glow temporarily, or physically shield someone from a grasping hand.

Round 1: The Approach

The party descends from the ridge, crosses open ground, and enters the tannery marshes. They must reach the edge of the siege ring without drawing the full host’s attention.

Test Characteristic Description

Control your fear Presence The dead are right there. Suppress panic and keep moving quietly.
Move unseen Agility Slip between the bodies. Time your steps to the ring’s slow breathing.
