Read the pattern Reason The dead shift in waves. Predict the next gap and signal the group.
Suppress the mark Presence (Kaelen only) Kaelen’s ember-mark is drawing attention. Help her muffle the glow with mud, cloth, or will.

Narrative Interlude: Among the Dead

Between rounds, describe the horror of being inside the siege ring.

You’re among them now. The smell hits first — not rot, but cold. A deep, mineral cold, like the inside of a cave that has never seen sunlight.

They stand around you. Close enough to touch. A draugr in a farmer’s clothes, his jaw hanging loose, frost threading through his beard. A woman in a burial shift, her eyes blank white, her hands curled around nothing. A child.

None of them look at you.

Kaelen has her hand pressed over her forehead so hard her knuckles are white. The ember-mark pulses beneath her fingers like a second heartbeat.

"Keep. Moving," Lew hisses.

Combat Complication: The Watchers

milestones — Type: Combat (Optional - mid-montage complication) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

A cluster of Frost-Ghouls — faster, hungrier dead that have been circling the siege ring’s second chance (two rolls, take best) like sharks — detect the party’s warmth. They don’t raise an alarm (the dead don’t communicate that way), but they attack.

Difficulty: Moderate (Level 3 PCs)

Special Constraint: This fight must be fast and quiet. If it drags past 3 rounds, the siege ring begins to close — see "Noise Penalty" below.

Enemies:

  • 4 Frost-Ghouls (Level 2 Standard)

Terrain:

  • Tannery channels (1-feet (2 m)-wide trenches, 4 feet deep, filled with icy chemical runoff — creature that falls in takes 3 cold damage and is restrained until they spend a move combat action to climb out)

Frost-Ghoul (Level 2 Standard)

Undead, Medium

Toughness: 24 | Speed: 7 (unusually fast) | Size: M | Saving Throws: 0

Characteristics: Might +2, Agility +4, Reason −2, Intuition +3, Presence −1

Round 2: The Final Sprint

After the ghoul fight, the party is close to the walls but exposed. The siege ring has shifted — the gap is closing.

Test Characteristic Description

Total Success (5+ successes, 0 failures): The party reaches the postern gate undetected. Guards open it immediately — they recognize the King’s cipher. The group enters without combat. +1 Victory.

Partial Success (reached success limit with 1 failure): The party reaches the gate, but the commotion draws a secondary wave. Hrafnborg’s defenses are weakened — −1 to the city’s Defence Track (see Part 5).

Failure (reached failure limit before success): The postern route collapses. The dead surge. The party fights a running battle to the main gate, where Harald orders a sortie to pull them in. Each PC takes 8 damage. The sortie costs lives — 6 Hrafnborg guards die. Harald’s anger and grief set the tone for the reunion scene. −2 to the city’s Defence Track.

Part 3: Inside the Walls

First Impressions

Hrafnborg under siege is a different city from the one the PCs left.

The city you remember is gone.

The streets are half-empty. The market feet (2 m) has been converted into a triage station — rows of cots under canvas, tended by exhausted healers. The Chainwardens' ledger-house has a queue stretching around the block: ration distribution.

Children don’t play. Dogs don’t bark. The only constant sound is the wind and the low, persistent hum of the Ever-Ember — a sound you can feel in your teeth more than hear.

Guards walk the walls in double shifts. Their faces are hollow. Several wear bandages. One is missing an arm.

And over everything, the Ever-Ember burns — small, orange, and tired. It used to light the whole city. Now its glow barely reaches the second ring of buildings.

Details of the Siege

If the PCs investigate or ask questions:

  • Duration: The siege began four days ago. The dead appeared overnight — no warning, no scouts, just a ring of corpses at dawn.

  • Attacks: Three probing assaults so far. The dead push against the walls in waves, then retreat. Each wave is slightly larger. The guards have held, but at cost.

  • Supplies: Two weeks of rationed food. Water is fine (wells are inside the walls). Fuel is the crisis — the Ever-Ember consumes fuel faster as it weakens, and the supply caravans are cut off.

  • Morale: Terrible. Rumours range from "the gods have abandoned us" to "the King caused this" to "the dead want someone specific." That last one is uncomfortably close to the truth.

  • The Pale Maw: Active inside the city. Preaching that resistance is futile, that the world should be allowed to end cleanly. Harald has arrested two of their agitators, which only makes them louder.

Hrafnborg Defence Track

Use a simple Defence Track to represent the city’s ability to hold. Start at 10. Reduce by the montage failure penalty (if any). Further reductions happen during the wall breach in Part 5.

Track Value City Status
10-8 Holding. Guards are tired but disciplined. Walls intact.
7-5 Strained. Sections of wall require reinforcement. Rationing tightened.
4-2 Critical. Breaches patched with rubble. Fires on the wall-walk. Civilian casualties.
1-0 Falling. The dead are inside the second ring. Evacuation to the Ember chamber.

This track is a GM tool — the PCs don’t see the number, but they see the consequences.

Reunion with Harald

Harald meets the PCs in person — not in his sitting room this time, but in the wall-walk command post, a fortified tower overlooking the main gate.

Harald is armoured now. Not the simple wool tunic of your first meeting — iron mail, scarred leather, a sword at his hip that has clearly seen use. His face is gaunt. His eyes are harder.

When he sees you, something cracks behind those eyes. Relief, quickly mastered.

"You made it." His gaze moves to Kaelen. He studies her for a long moment — the ember-mark on her forehead, barely visible beneath road-grime and ash.

"Keeper," he says. Not a question. An acknowledgment.

Kaelen meets his eyes. "Highness."

The silence holds for three heartbeats.

"Then there’s still a chance," Harald says. And for the first time, his voice shakes.

Harald’s briefing (summarise or roleplay as needed):

  • The siege is tightening. He expects a major assault within two days.

  • The Ever-Ember is failing faster. The Keepers' absence has accelerated the decline — the flame has no one to speak to it, tend it, or calm it.

  • He has been unable to reach any other settlements. Hrafnborg is alone.

  • He asks Kaelen directly: "Can you speak to the flame?"

Kaelen’s answer: "I haven’t spoken to an Ember in twelve years. I don’t know if it will still recognise me. But I have to try."

Lew’s Goodbye (Optional)

If the players have bonded with Lew, give him a departure scene. Lew is not a warrior — he’s a finder, and his part of the bargain is complete. He delivered the party to Kaelen and guided them home.

Lew pulls you aside while Kaelen and Harald talk logistics.

"I’ve done what I said I’d do. Found your Keeper. Walked your road. Nearly died twice — three times if you count the mud." He taps the amber eye. "This thing is going dark. Has been since we left the Green Heart. Whatever’s happening with the tree… it’s pulling everything inward."

He holds out his hand. "You owe me a favour. I intend to collect. But first, you have to survive this."

His grin is thin but real.

"Try not to die. Dead men are terrible at paying debts."

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Lew’s Status

Lew can remain in Hrafnborg as a background NPC, or he can depart before the wall breach (slipping out through a route "only I know about"). His role in Act 2 depends on campaign direction:

  • If Lew stays: He becomes an information broker inside the besieged city, feeding the PCs intel during Act 2’s early planning phase.

  • If Lew leaves: He surfaces later as a surprise ally or complication — perhaps he’s found something worse on the road, or perhaps Grafvitnir’s agents have found him.

Either way, the favour remains uncollected. Plant this seed.

Part 4: The Ever-Ember (Ritual Scene)

This is the emotional and narrative core of Beat 9. Kaelen attempts to speak to the Ever-Ember — the first time a Keeper has addressed the flame in over a year.

The Ember Chamber

The Ever-Ember burns in a stone chamber beneath Hrafnborg’s central hall. The room is ancient — older than the city above it, carved from the living rock of the fjord.

The stairway descends into warmth. Not comfortable warmth — desperate warmth. The heat of a dying coal giving everything it has left.

The Ember Chamber is circular, thirty feet across, with a domed ceiling carved with runes so old they’ve smoothed to shapes. At the centre, on a ring of blackened stone, the Ever-Ember burns.

It is small. Smaller than you expected. A flame the size of a man’s fist, dancing on a bed of white ash. Its light is orange, tinged with grey at the edges. The shadows it casts are too long, and they move wrong — stretching toward the flame instead of away from it.

The air tastes of ash and something older. Something like sap, or resin, or the memory of a forest that no longer exists.

Seven stone seats ring the flame. Six are empty. The seventh — cracked, its surface scarred with the same ember-mark that Kaelen bears — waits.

The Ritual

Kaelen approaches the flame-ring. She removes her cloak, her gloves, her boots. Barefoot on the cold stone, she sits in the cracked seventh seat.

Kaelen closes her eyes. The ember-mark on her forehead ignites — not the faint pulse of before, but a steady, painful glow. Her face tightens. Her hands grip the stone armrests until her knuckles whiten.

"I’m here," she whispers. "I left, and I’m sorry, and I’m here."

The Ever-Ember flickers. Then, slowly, it reaches. A tendril of flame stretches from the bed of ash toward Kaelen. Not heat — not light — but connection. The flame touches her mark.

Kaelen screams.

Not in pain. In grief.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Running the Ritual

This scene is narrative, not mechanical. Do not roll dice. Let the players watch. Their characters can be present in the chamber, and they can react, but they cannot help — this is between Kaelen and the flame.

The emotions Kaelen experiences (and transmits through the chamber’s resonance) are:

  1. Loneliness. The flame has been alone. No one has spoken to it. It has been screaming into silence for a year.

  2. Exhaustion. The flame is not dying by choice. It is being drained. Something is pulling its warmth downward, through the roots, into the deep dark.

  3. Memory. Flashes of the World-Tree — Yggdrasil as it once was: alive, green, vast, its roots threading through every stone in Svellheim. And Yggdrasil as it is now: grey, hollow, its sap turned to bitter resin, its roots cracking.

  4. A name. Not spoken — felt. Grafvitnir. The Grave-Wolf, gnawing at the deepest root. Not free yet. But close. So close.

  5. A direction. Downward. Northward. To the place where the first Ember was lit. The Emberwell.

This is the campaign’s pivot. Let it breathe. Let Kaelen cry. Let the players sit with what the flame is showing them.

After the Ritual

Kaelen slumps in the stone seat. The ember-mark dims to its usual faint glow. The Ever-Ember is slightly brighter — not healed, but heard. Speaking to it has given the flame strength enough to last weeks instead of days.

When Kaelen speaks, her voice is raw.

"It’s not dying. It’s being eaten."

She looks at you with red-rimmed eyes.

"Yggdrasil. The World-Tree. The Ever-Embers draw their power from its roots — always have. Sap, warmth, life — it all flows upward from the tree into the flames. But something is consuming the tree from below. Gnawing through the deepest root."

Her voice drops.

"Grafvitnir. The name Gragnir gave us. The Grave-Wolf. It’s real. It’s been imprisoned in the roots since the First Kindling, and every Ember that dies is a lock that opens. When the last Ember goes dark…"

She doesn’t finish the sentence.

"The flame showed me a place. The Emberwell. It’s where the first Ember was lit — where Yggdrasil’s deepest root surfaces. If the tree can be renewed anywhere, it’s there."

She stands. Her legs shake.

"But it’s far. South, beyond the Interior Belt, beyond the White Waste. At the Throat of the World."

Allow the PCs to ask questions. Kaelen can answer:

  • What is the Emberwell? "The First Hearth. Where the World-Tree’s root breaks the surface. Where someone — the stories don’t say who — lit the first flame and the Embers spread outward through the roots to every settlement."

  • Can you heal the tree? "I don’t know. The flame showed me the place. It didn’t show me how. Maybe when we get there, we’ll understand."

  • What about Hrafnborg? "The Ember is stronger now. Not much, but enough. Speaking to it bought time. Maybe a month, if nothing gets worse." She pauses. "But things will get worse."

  • What about Grafvitnir? "It’s not free. Not yet. But it’s aware. It felt me speak to the Ember. It knows someone is listening now." She touches her ember-mark. "And I think it’s angry."

Part 5: The Breach (Boss Combat)

milestones — Type: Combat (Boss) | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 2 VP on victory

Kaelen’s connection to the Ember did not go unnoticed. The ritual sent a pulse of warmth through Yggdrasil’s roots — a flare in the dark. The dead felt it. Grafvitnir felt it. And now the siege army moves.

The Alarm

This can trigger immediately after the ritual or after a brief rest (GM’s choice based on pacing). Alarm bells ring across Hrafnborg.

The bells start on the western wall. Then the eastern. Then everywhere.

You reach the ramparts and see it: the silent ring has broken. The dead are no longer waiting.

They come in a single, massive wave — not at the gate, but at the southwestern wall. The section weakened by the marshes. The section you came through.

At the front of the tide, something larger. A figure in corroded mail, bearing a banner of frozen bone. A crown of black iron sits on its skull. It walks upright, deliberate, and the dead part around it.

A Wight-Lord. And it’s heading for the breach.

Scene Setup

Combat: The Wall Breach

Difficulty: Hard (Level 3 PCs, climactic scene)

Environment: Hrafnborg’s southwestern wall section. The battle takes place on and around the wall-walk, with the dead surging below.

Map Features:

  • Wall-walk (2 feet wide, elevated 15 feet): The main fighting platform. PCs and guards hold this line.

  • Breach point (3 feet wide): A section of wall that has partially collapsed from the marsh erosion. The dead climb through here. The Wight-Lord targets this point.

  • Inner courtyard (below the wall, open ground): If the dead get through, civilians and the wounded are exposed.

  • Signal-fire braziers (3 locations on the wall): Bright light in 4 feet. Can be toppled onto enemies below (Combat Action: easy Might test, deals 8 fire damage in a 2×2 area, one-time use per brazier).

  • Oil trenches (1 feet (2 m) wide, along the base of the wall): Pre-prepared defence. Can be ignited (Combat Action: touch with any fire source, burns for 3 rounds, deals 5 fire damage to any creature entering or starting its turn in the trench).

Allied Forces:

  • 6 Hrafnborg Guards (Level 1 Minions, Toughness 1, Speed 5, Spear +3 vs Might for 3 damage). They hold the flanks. When a guard dies, describe it — these are named people with families.

  • Harald is present but commanding the defence at other wall sections. He does not fight here unless the Defence Track drops to 0.

  • Kaelen is exhausted from the ritual. She can take one combat action per round (either a melee attack at +4 for 6 damage, or use her ember-mark to create bright light in a 3-feet (2 m) radius, giving the undead a second chance (two rolls, take worst) on attacks within it). She cannot sustain this past 3 rounds.

Enemies:

  • 1 Wight-Lord (Level 4 Brute Elite)

  • 10 Draugr Thralls (Level 1 Brute Minion)

  • 4 Oath-Broken Draugr (Level 3 Brute Elite)

Reinforcements: At the start of round 3 and each subsequent round, 2 additional Draugr Thralls climb through the breach. This stops when the Wight-Lord is destroyed (its command aura sustains the assault).

Wight-Lord (Level 4 Boss)

Undead, Medium

Toughness: 80 | Speed: 5 | Size: M | Saving Throws: 2 | Free Attack: 6

Characteristics: Might +4, Agility +1, Reason +2, Intuition +3, Presence +4

Immunities: Fear, charm, poison, cold

Attacks:

  • Fellblade (Melee, Might, Signature): +6 vs. Might; 4/7/10 damage. On tier 3: target takes ongoing 3 cold damage (save ends) and is frightened of the Wight-Lord (save ends).

  • Command the Fallen (Combat Action, 3 Malice, Range 10): The Wight-Lord raises 2 Draugr Thralls in unoccupied feet within range. These thralls act immediately with a single move combat action.

  • Death-Gaze (Combat Action, Range 5, Presence): 6 vs. Presence; 6 psychic damage. On tier 2: the target is dazed (save ends). On tier 3: the target is instead stunned (save ends).

Triggered Actions:

  • Undying Will (Triggered, 1/scene): When reduced to 0 Toughness, the Wight-Lord does not fall. Instead, it is reduced to 1 Toughness, gains resist 5 to all damage until the end of its next turn, and makes a free Fellblade attack against the nearest creature. It can be killed normally after this.

Traits:

  • Lord of the Barrow: All allied undead within 5 feet gain +1 to damage rolls and cannot be turned or banished while the Wight-Lord lives.

  • Crown of Black Iron: The Wight-Lord continually emanates supernatural cold. Any creature that ends its turn adjacent to the Wight-Lord takes 2 cold damage.

  • Barrow-Sense: The Wight-Lord can sense living creatures within 10 feet regardless of concealment or invisibility.

Malice Abilities (Director spends Malice):

  • Grave-Command (2 Malice): All Draugr Thralls within 10 feet immediately shift 2 feet and make a Free Attack.

  • Frost Roar (3 Malice): Each creature within 3 feet must make a medium Might test or be pushed 2 feet and take 4 cold damage. On a tier 1 result, they are also knocked prone. The breach point widens — treat the breach as 1 feet (2 m) wider for the rest of the scene.

Appearance: A towering figure in corroded mail, its skull crowned with black iron. The banner it carries is a human spine wrapped in frozen sinew. Its eye sockets burn with pale green fire. When it speaks, its voice is the grinding of stone on stone.

Oath-Broken Draugr (Level 3 Standard)

Undead, Medium

Toughness: 40 | Speed: 5 | Size: M | Saving Throws: 1 | Free Attack: 5

Characteristics: Might +3, Agility +2, Reason +1, Intuition +2, Presence +1

Attacks:

  • Oath-Blade (Melee, Might): +5 vs. Might; 7 damage. On tier 3: target takes ongoing 2 cold damage (save ends).

  • Accusing Howl (Combat Action, 5 Malice, Range 5, Presence): +4 vs. Presence; 5 psychic damage and target has a second chance (two rolls, take worst) on their next ability check. (The draugr screams a name — not yours, but someone you’ve failed.)

Traits:

  • Tactical Dead: The Oath-Broken fights with remembered skill. It flanks, retreats from fire, and targets isolated PCs.

  • Unfeeling: Immune to fear, charm, and pain-based effects.

  • The Dead Remember: When the Oath-Broken drops to 0 Toughness, it whispers one true thing — a fragment of memory from its former life. This has no mechanical effect but can provide lore clues or emotional weight.

Appearance: Better-preserved than thralls. Wears the remains of a hirdman’s armour. Its eyes hold a dull, hateful intelligence.

Combat Narrative Beats

Use these to structure the battle’s emotional arc:

Round 1-2: The Surge. The dead pour through the breach. The wall guards hold the flanks. The PCs face the Oath-Broken vanguard.

The first draugr through the breach is a farmer. You can see the calluses on its hands. It lurches at the nearest guard with dead fingers.

Behind it: soldiers. Real soldiers, once — their armour is wrong, their movements are wrong, but the muscle memory of shield-walls is still there. They come through the breach in formation.

Round 3-4: The Lord Arrives. The Wight-Lord reaches the breach. When it steps onto the wall-walk, the temperature drops. Breath fogs. Weapons frost over.

The Wight-Lord places one mailed hand on the broken wall and pulls itself up. Stone cracks under its grip. The banner of frozen bone plants itself in the rubble, and the dead around it straighten. Their shuffling stops. Their movements become purposeful.

The Wight-Lord opens its jaw and speaks. The voice is old and full of gravel and buried things.

"The Keeper has spoken to the flame. The flame has answered. And the answer is: it is too late."

It draws its blade — a black iron thing, sheeted in ice — and points it at your chest.

"Give us the Keeper. Let the rest end."

Round 5+: Desperation. The battle turns on killing the Wight-Lord. When it uses Undying Will, the dead surge. When it finally falls —

The Wight-Lord falls. Its crown cracks against the stone, splitting into three pieces. The black iron corrodes to dust in seconds.

For a heartbeat, the dead freeze. Then — like puppets with cut strings — they slump. Not all of them. Not even most. But the coordination breaks. The wave falters. Guards shout, surge forward, push the thralls back through the breach.

Somewhere on the wall, someone starts hammering. Rubble goes into the gap. The line holds.

Barely.

Aftermath

After the combat:

  • The breach is sealed with rubble. It will hold — for now.

  • The Hrafnborg Defence Track drops by 1 (or more, if guards died or the breach widened via Frost Roar).

  • The dead outside resume their vigil. They don’t retreat, but the coordinated assault stops with the Wight-Lord’s death.

  • If any named guards died, describe the aftermath. Hrafnborg cannot afford losses.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Consequences and the Defence Track

The current Defence Track value determines the tone of the Act 1 ending:

  • 8+: Hrafnborg is hurting but hopeful. The breach held. The PCs are heroes. Harald speaks of the mission to the Emberwell with cautious optimism.

  • 5-7: The city is wounded. The PCs are respected but feared — "they brought the Keeper, and the dead attacked harder." Harald speaks of the Emberwell as a last option.

  • Below 5: Hrafnborg is on its knees. The PCs see stretchers, mourning, accusation. Harald orders the mission to the Emberwell not as hope but as the only thing left.

Let the track inform the emotional register of Part 6 and 7. The mission is the same regardless — but how the PCs feel about it changes everything.

Part 5b: The Shape-Walker’s Move

ðŸ“ Director Note: This Scene Interrupts Part 5

This scene is triggered during the wall breach combat — specifically at the start of Round 3, when the Wight-Lord reaches the breach and the battle is at its most chaotic. The party is split between holding the wall and saving the King. This is intentional. It is the hardest choice in Act 1.

The Trigger

At the start of Round 3 of the wall combat, a runner arrives from the keep interior. He is young, bleeding from a gash across his forehead, and his voice cracks:

“The King’s chambers — something’s wrong — we heard screaming, the guards aren’t answering, the door won’t—”

He grabs the nearest PC’s arm.

“The hound. Someone said they saw the hound walk upright.”

The party now has a decision that cannot wait: the Wight-Lord is on the wall and the wall will not hold itself. But Harald is alone in the King’s chambers and Dreyfus has finally moved.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: The Split

The intent is to force hard choices, not punish bad luck. Structure the split as follows:

If the party splits: Some PCs stay to fight the Wight-Lord (the wall cannot hold without them — guards are Level 1 minions and Kaelen is exhausted). Some PCs go to Harald. Both tracks run simultaneously. Use a simple countdown: every 2 rounds that pass without the King’s group reaching Harald, Harald’s Toughness track drops by 1 step (see the table below).

If the entire party goes to Harald: The Wight-Lord kills 2 guards per round and begins driving the dead deeper into the city. Defence Track drops by 1 per round until the Wight-Lord is destroyed. The party will have to defeat Dreyfus and then return to finish the Wight-Lord (it will still be present, directing the assault, having killed several guards).

If the entire party stays on the wall: Harald’s Toughness track depletes at 1 step per round. At track 0, Harald is gravely wounded — he survives, but barely, and he will carry the injury into Act 2. More importantly: Dreyfus escapes before the party can confront him, having completed his mission of intel-gathering and attempted to cripple the crown. He will not be seen again until Act 3.

Harald’s Toughness Track

TrackStatus
3Harald is cornered but unhurt. Dreyfus is circling, waiting for an opening.
2Harald has a wound — cut across the arm, blood on the stones. He’s fighting with a chair. Dreyfus is pressing the second chance (two rolls, take best).
1Harald is down. Unconscious but alive. Dreyfus is 1 round from finishing the job when the PCs arrive.
0Harald is dead. This ends the campaign’s political structure in Hrafnborg. It is survivable as a campaign consequence, but it changes everything. Run it only if the choices truly led here.

Dreyfus: The King’s Chambers

The door to the King’s inner chambers is splintered from inside. The two guards posted here are not dead — they are pressed against the wall in a fugue, eyes wide and empty, breathing but absent. Something about the shape-walker’s presence hollows people who aren’t protected by guest-right.

Inside: Harald is backed against his war-table. He’s holding a hunting knife in one hand and a candlestick in the other, and he is absolutely, unmistakably furious rather than afraid.

Across from him stands something that used to be a grey hound.

It is no longer a grey hound.

ðŸ“ GM Notes: Dreyfus’s Revealed Form

Dreyfus is a shape-walker — Níðhöggr’s eyes and ears given mortal form, serving through Grafvitnir’s network. His revealed shape is not a monster. That would be too easy.

He is a man. Or the idea of a man: tall, grey-pale, features that are almost-right in a way that the eye cannot fix on. His face shifts slightly when you look directly at him, as if it hasn’t decided what to settle on. His clothes are Harald’s hound-keeper’s livery, but they fit too well for a dog that just transformed. He has been wearing them for months, in his mind.

His eyes are wrong. They are gold, and they are too still. They do not blink.

When he speaks, his voice is pleasant. Educated. The voice of a court advisor.

“You made better time than expected,” he says. He does not move. He is not afraid. He is calculating.

Dreyfus, Shape-Walker Agent (Level 4 Ambusher Elite)

Humanoid (Wyrd-bound), Medium

Toughness: 60 | Speed: 7 | Size: M | Saving Throws: 1 | Free Attack: 6

Characteristics: Might +1, Agility +4, Reason +3, Intuition +4, Presence +3

Immunities: Fear, charm

Attacks:

  • Wyrd-Claw (Melee, Agility, Signature): +6 vs. Agility; 5/8/11 damage. On tier 2+: the target has a second chance (two rolls, take worst) on their next ability check (the wound carries the shape-walker’s Wyrd-taint). On tier 3: the target is also weakened until the end of their next turn.
  • Hollowing Gaze (Combat Action, Range 5, Intuition, 3 Malice): +5 vs. Presence; 6 psychic damage. On tier 2+: target is dazed (save ends). On tier 3: target is stunned until end of their next turn and loses their next combat action as they are flooded with a false memory. (The false memory is mundane and disorienting, not horrific — something ordinary that shouldn’t be there.)

Triggered Actions:

  • Shape-Shift (Triggered, 1/round): When Dreyfus takes 10+ damage from a single attack, he may immediately shift his form, becoming a Medium beast (dog, raven, or large rat). He moves up to 3 feet without provoking free strikes. He reverts to his humanoid form at the start of his next turn or when he chooses. While in beast form, he cannot attack but cannot be targeted by effects that require a humanoid target.

Traits:

  • Wyrd-Guest: Dreyfus is bound by the laws of guest-right. He cannot make the first attack on anyone he is bound to by guest-right within a space he has been invited into. Harald invited him inside as his hound. Dreyfus has been manoeuvering for weeks to get Harald to make a hostile move first — which Harald just did with the hunting knife. The guest-right is now void. (This matters for future encounters: if the PCs ever invite Dreyfus in anywhere, note it.)
  • Intelligence Asset: Dreyfus has been listening for months. He knows the PCs’ strengths, fighting styles, and likely tactics. At the start of combat, he identifies the hero with the highest Presence and the hero with the highest Might. He targets the Presence-highest first (most dangerous in conversation) and avoids melee with the Might-highest unless cornered.
  • Escape Protocol: When Dreyfus is reduced to half Toughness or below, he immediately uses Shape-Shift to take raven form and attempts to flee through the nearest window, chimney, or gap. He uses his full movement to exit the building. He cannot be stopped by free strikes in raven form (he is too fast and too small). He escapes. This is intentional — he has more appearances ahead.

Malice Abilities:

  • Grafvitnir’s Shadow (3 Malice): All creatures within 3 feet must make a medium Intuition test or lose track of Dreyfus for 1 round — he seems to be somewhere he isn’t. Effectively grants him concealment for 1 round.
  • Root-Whisper (1 Malice, triggered on any successful Wyrd-Claw): Dreyfus relays what he just learned about the target through Yggdrasil’s dying root network. This has no immediate mechanical effect, but the GM should note it: Grafvitnir now knows which PC Dreyfus wounded and gains information about their condition.

Appearance: In humanoid form: tall, grey-pale, almost-right features, gold unblinking eyes, Harald’s hound-keeper livery. In beast form: a large grey dog with gold eyes, silent movement, and the unsettling habit of looking directly at the person speaking rather than moving the way a dog moves.

The Fight

This is not a dungeon scene. It is a tight, fast scene in the King’s inner chamber — a private room with a war-table, a fireplace, a locked chest, and one narrow window.

Space constraints: The room holds 4–5 creatures comfortably. The war-table is difficult terrain if fighting across it. The fireplace is a hazard (1 feet (2 m), 3 fire damage to any creature that enters or is pushed into it).

Harald’s role: He is not a combatant. He will use the hunting knife if cornered, but do not let him die here unless the defence track forced it. If a PC gets between Harald and Dreyfus, Harald will try to get to a guard position or behind cover. He watches everything with the expression of a man cataloguing the worst day of his reign.

Dreyfus’s goal: He is not trying to destroy the party. He was trying to kill Harald before anyone arrived. Now that the party is here, he wants to escape with maximum information. He fights to survive, not to win. He will attempt Hollowing Gaze on the most dangerous-looking PC to buy himself room, then try to wound Harald one more time, then escape.

When Dreyfus flees: He shifts to raven form and goes through the window — or up the chimney if the window is blocked. The raven form is fast enough to dodge a thrown object (difficult Agility test to hit him). When he is gone:

The raven hits the window ledge, folds — and then it’s just a grey shape dropping into the dark over the siege ring.

Something small clatters to the stone floor. A talon — curved, black as obsidian, still warm. It fell from the raven’s grasp during the shift, torn free by the violence of the transformation.

Then the raven is gone.

Harald lowers the knife. His hand is shaking, though his face has gone very still.

“I invited that thing into my hall,” he says. His voice is very quiet. “I let it sleep at my feet.”

He looks at the window for a long time.

“How long has it been listening?”

ðŸ“ Director Notes: Managing Dreyfus’s Designed Escape

Why this escape serves the story. Dreyfus is not a one-beat villain. His escape here creates a campaign-long shadow — every settlement, every exposed campfire, every intelligence failure in Act 2 carries the weight of knowing he is out in the world with everything he learned. When the party finally faces him in Beat 24, that confrontation is earned across fifteen beats of hatred and dread. Killing him here would remove one of the campaign’s most effective sources of tension.

Making the escape feel earned, not arbitrary. The party should never feel cheated. Ensure the following before Dreyfus flees:

  • The party hurts him. They must deal real damage — ideally reducing him to his escape threshold through their own skill. The escape triggers at half Toughness, not at the start of the fight.
  • They save Harald. Even though Dreyfus escapes, the King is alive because the party acted. That is their victory.
  • They get the talon. The Shape-Walker’s Talon (see items-act1.adoc) is physical proof they drew blood. It is their trophy and a tool they can use later.
  • Harald’s reaction grounds the moment in consequence, not frustration. The read-aloud above gives Harald shame and fury — that emotional weight redirects the table’s energy from “he got away” to “look what he did to us.”

Handling clever players. If players try to trap Dreyfus (blocking the window, grappling in human form, using abilities to pin him):

  • Reward the thinking. Acknowledge the clever play. If they block the window, he tries the chimney. If they block both, he burns Grafvitnir’s Shadow (3 Malice) to create confusion and finds a gap — the Wyrd gives him spatial awareness the party cannot match in an enclosed space.
  • Give them something extra. If the party makes a genuinely brilliant play (successful hard Intuition test to anticipate his escape route, or dealing enough damage in a single round to nearly kill him), grant the talon automatically (no damage threshold required) and add a detail: the raven that escapes is trailing feathers, one wing dragging. They almost had him. That “almost” is worth more narratively than a clean kill.
  • Do not let them kill him. If by extraordinary luck or creative play they reduce him to 0 Toughness before the escape triggers, he still shifts — but the transformation is ragged, painful, and visibly costs him. He escapes but he is broken. This will show when he reappears in Beat 24. Note exactly what the party did to him, because the Director should describe those specific injuries manifesting as degradation in his form fifteen beats later.

Foreshadowing the cost. Whatever damage the party deals in this fight leaves a mark. Write down the specific injuries: which PC wounded him, with what weapon, where the blow landed. In Beat 24, when Dreyfus reappears with “edges that flicker” and shifting that is “less clean,” reference those specific wounds. The party should see the evidence of what they did to him here — proof that their actions in Beat 9 mattered even though he escaped.

Aftermath: Dreyfus’s Information

The PCs know, and Harald now knows: Dreyfus was Grafvitnir’s agent for the entirety of their mission. Everything discussed in Hrafnborg — the mission to find Kaelen, the route to the Green Heart, the party’s capabilities — was relayed through Wyrd-channels to Grafvitnir’s network.

What Dreyfus did not know (because it happened after he fled the chambers): the full contents of Kaelen’s ritual communication with the Ember. He was outside the Ember Chamber’s Wyrd-ward when it occurred. Grafvitnir knows Kaelen spoke to the flame, and knows it made the flame stronger — but not what was said or where the party is going.

This is the party’s one intelligence second chance (two rolls, take best). They know where they’re going. Grafvitnir does not — yet.

Part 6: What the Ember Said

After the battle, in a quieter moment, Kaelen and the PCs convene with Harald in the Ember Chamber. The flame is steadier now — Kaelen’s communion stabilised it — but the fundamental truth is clear.

Kaelen’s Report

Kaelen stands before the flame. She is bruised, exhausted, and still trembling from the battle. But her voice is steady.

"The Ever-Embers are not hearth-fires. They never were. They’re leaves. Yggdrasil’s warmth flows upward through the roots, surfaces at the Embers, and spreads outward. Every Ember is a node in a living system — warmth, life, the boundary between the living and the dead. All of it flows from the tree."

She touches the flame with one bare hand. It doesn’t burn her.

"Yggdrasil is dying. Something — Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf — has been gnawing at the deepest root since the First Kindling. It was imprisoned there as part of the binding. The prison is the root. And as the root weakens, the Embers fed by it — all of them — are guttering."

She looks at Harald.

"The flame showed me the Emberwell. The place where the first Ember was kindled, where the root surfaces. If the tree can be healed — or the prison reforged — it has to happen there."

"But the flame also showed me Grafvitnir. Not clearly. Just… hunger. An intelligence that has been patient for an age, and whose patience is ending."

She takes a breath.

"If we do nothing, the last Ember dies within the year. Grafvitnir breaks free. And then…"

She doesn’t finish.

Harald’s Decision

Harald’s response depends on the Defence Track and the tone of the session, but the core decision is the same:

Harald is silent for a long time. When he speaks, his voice is that of a king delivering a sentence — not of hope, but of law.

"I cannot lead this mission. Hrafnborg needs a king on the walls. But I can give you what I have: supplies, maps, authority."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"You’ve proven yourselves. You found Kaelen. You broke the siege line. You held the wall. I trust you with what comes next — not because I have faith, but because I have no other option."

He draws a deep breath.

"Go to the Emberwell. Find out what the tree needs. Do what must be done."

His voice cracks, just once.

"And come back."

Gragnir’s Final Words

If the GM wishes, Gragnir appears one last time — at the edge of the Ember Chamber, in the doorway, half in shadow. He does not enter. The Ever-Ember’s light seems to bend around him.

"The flame told her true." Gragnir’s voice is low, rough, and carries the smell of peat-smoke. "The Emberwell is real. The road is south, through the White Waste, to the Throat of the World."

He pauses.

"I cannot walk that road with you. Not all of it. The closer we get to the deep root, the more Grafvitnir will feel me — and I have kept my presence hidden for too long to burn that second chance (two rolls, take best) now."

His gaze moves to Kaelen. Something passes between them — old pain, old debt.

"But I will find you when it matters. I always do."

He turns to leave. Then stops.

"The shape-walker — Dreyfus — is still at Harald’s side. He heard every plan you made before you left, and he has been listening to every word the King has spoken since. When you return, the hound will be there, watching. You must decide what to do about that — but do not act rashly. Guest-right binds even shape-walkers, and a cornered spy is more dangerous than a patient one."

A half-smile, ash-dry.

"Trust is the only currency left that doesn’t freeze."

He is gone. The doorway is empty. The ash-smell lingers.

Part 7: The Road Ahead (Act 2 Hook)

The final scene of Act 1 sets the stage for the next chapter.

Preparations

Give the PCs a short downtime scene in Hrafnborg before departure. They can:

  • Resupply: Harald provides cold-weather gear, rations (10 days), maps of the Interior Belt and the northern routes toward the White Waste, and a sealed letter of authority bearing the Cold Iron Crown’s seal.

  • Gather information: Ask NPCs about the Emberwell, the White Waste, the Throat of the World. Most people have never heard of the Emberwell specifically, but the Throat of the World is known in legend — a volcanic plateau beyond the northern glaciers where "the roots of the world break the ice."

  • Personal scenes: Say goodbye to NPCs, write letters, make promises. If Lew is still in Hrafnborg, he makes a final snide comment and refuses to say anything sentimental.

  • Rest: A extended rest in actual beds. This may be the last time for a while.

The Departure

You leave Hrafnborg at dawn. The guards open the postern gate — the same one you entered through — and you slip out into the grey.

The dead are still there. But they’ve pulled back from the southwestern marsh. Not far. Just enough. Kaelen’s ember-mark glows faintly, and the nearest draugr flinch.

It won’t last. The mark’s strength will fade as you move further from the Ever-Ember. But for now, it clears a path.

The road north stretches ahead of you. The Interior Belt. The White Waste beyond it. And somewhere, at the end of the world, the Emberwell.

Behind you, Hrafnborg’s Ever-Ember flickers. Ahead, the wind rises.

You walk.

Outcomes

By the end of Beat 9, the PCs should have:

  • Broken through the undead siege line to reach Hrafnborg.

  • Witnessed the city under siege — the human cost of the crisis.

  • Watched Kaelen speak to the Ever-Ember and received the full revelation: Yggdrasil is dying, Grafvitnir is gnawing at the deepest root, the Embers are failing because the tree is failing.

  • Defended the walls against a Wight-Lord assault — the climactic combat of Act 1.

  • Received their new mission: journey to the Emberwell to renew the spark (or discover what must be done).

  • Said goodbye to Harald and (possibly) Lew.

  • Heard Gragnir’s final warning about the road ahead and the shape-walker still at Harald’s side.

  • Set out south toward the White Waste and the Throat of the World.
